title: mu-clip-coding-tests-deadcode: 2 dead_code in mu-coding test files (run_mu_ask, read_n_lines)
description:
Two dead_code warnings in mu-coding test files. Likely abandoned test helpers; probably delete (test fixtures that aren't called are pure noise).

## Items

1. `run_mu_ask` (`crates/mu-coding/tests/.../mod.rs:29`) — function defined but never called from any test.
2. `read_n_lines` (`crates/mu-coding/tests/serve_smoke.rs:62`) — async helper defined but never called.

## Decision

For each: either (a) delete if truly unused, OR (b) `#[allow(dead_code)]` if there's a recent reason to keep it (e.g. just removed the last caller and a future test will need it back).

Default: delete. Test code shouldn't accumulate dead helpers — VCS history holds the prior version if needed.

## Acceptance

- Both dead_code items addressed (deleted or allow-with-comment)
- cargo clippy passes (for these specific lints)
- cargo test --workspace green (especially: confirm no test depends transitively on either helper)
- One commit closes this bead
close_reason: Landed in jj revision vqnttvrx. read_n_lines deleted (no callers); run_mu_ask kept with #[allow(dead_code)] matching the existing convention for shared test helpers used by some-but-not-all binaries. cargo test --workspace 361 green; both dead_code lints gone.

title: mu-tui: set terminal title to μ - <username> on TUI startup; reset on exit
description:
Set the terminal/pane title to `μ - <username>` when mu's TUI enters alternate screen, mirroring pi_ts's `π - <username>` convention. Reset to empty on exit (alternate-screen leave, panic handler, normal shutdown).

## Scope

IN:
- One call in the TUI startup path (probably in `crates/mu-tui/` wherever the alternate-screen entry happens) using crossterm's `SetTitle` command. `SetTitle` wraps OSC 0 (`\x1b]0;TITLE\x07`), which zellij, kitty, tmux, foot, alacritty all honor.
- Symmetric reset on shutdown: `SetTitle("")` in the same place TUI tears down (and in the panic handler so an abnormal exit doesn't leave the title hanging).
- Username via the `whoami` crate (probably already in the dep tree) or `std::env::var("USER")`.
- A snapshot or byte-level test asserting the emitted escape-sequence shape if practical; otherwise rely on visual verification.

OUT:
- Dynamic title updates (e.g., showing current session id or model). v2 if useful.
- Title-setting from inside individual TUI panels (this is one global startup-time set).

## Acceptance

- Terminal title shows `μ - <username>` when running mu's TUI inside zellij/kitty
- Title resets cleanly on normal exit AND panic
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- pi_ts/pi_agent_rust pattern: cached JS module at `~/.pi/agent/cache/modules/...` calls `ctx.ui.setTitle(\`π - ${name}\`)` 150ms after some event. The Rust-side `apply_extension_title_effect` (`pi_agent_rust/src/interactive/agent.rs:1089`) only sets a status message — actual OSC emission likely happens in a JS layer or shell hook there. For mu, doing it directly in Rust via crossterm is cleaner.
- crossterm: `crossterm::terminal::SetTitle` is the API.
- Greek letter choice: μ (U+03BC, lowercase mu) parallels π exactly.

## Notes

Small in-context job (~10-15 lines including panic-handler reset). Not /goal-worthy unless folded into a larger TUI batch. Queued post-mu-cap-aws (experiment 6 in flight as of filing).
close_reason: Landed in jj revision nnnxloko. SetTitle on startup + reset on shutdown + re-set on $EDITOR resume. mu_terminal_title() helper uses USER with fallback. cargo fmt + clippy -D warnings + 361 tests green. Panic-handler cleanup deferred to a separate bead.

title: goal-pr-flow: agent /goal sessions work in jj workspaces + submit PRs instead of pushing direct-to-main
description:
Standing convention: every autonomous /goal session works in its own jj workspace (or git worktree), commits on a session-named branch, and ends by opening a PR against `main` — NOT by pushing direct-to-main. The operator merges (or rejects) the PR after CI validates.

## Why

The capability-broker pattern (memory `03b44633`) names *two-person control* as the structural property: single-actor compromise cannot grow scope. PR + merge maps that pattern onto the workflow layer:

| PR-flow | Capability-broker |
|---|---|
| Agent in its own workspace | Worker session attenuated at spawn |
| Branch + commit + push | Agent produces signed work order |
| PR + diff review | Judge inspects the proposal |
| CI runs against the PR | Orchestrator's policy verifies content |
| Operator merge | Orchestrator dispatches |
| Direct-to-main = unconstrained operator-only exception | Unconstrained operator-only path |

The CI we landed via `mu-jza` (workflow triggers on both `pull_request` AND `push: main`) sets the stage perfectly. PR-shaped work gets validated at the gate; the operator's merge button is the two-person-control point.

## Practical benefits

- **Working-copy isolation**: each session has its own `@`. Eliminates cross-revision pollution we hit yesterday (mu-ovl-A worker accumulating into experiment-3's briefing commit; multiple jj-mixing surprises).
- **Reviewable atomic units**: the diff IS the artifact. Operator sees the change before it lands.
- **Same workflow as human collaborators**: no special agent flow, no special agent commands. PR is PR.
- **Reversibility**: PRs close cleanly without merging; direct-to-main commits can only be reverted (visible in history).
- **CI surfaces failures BEFORE merge**: today's direct-to-main + CI-runs-after-push pattern means failed CI on main → ugly recovery. PR-flow + CI-before-merge means failed CI on PR → just close it.

## Concrete pieces required

1. **Per-session workspace setup helper** — wrapper around `jj workspace add` that creates a fresh checkout at `/tmp/<repo>-session-<id>/` or similar. Goal-protocol skill ensures the autonomous /goal session lands in this isolated workspace before any edits.

2. **Branch naming convention** — `agent/<session-id>-<short-slug>` or `agent/<date>-<bead-id>`. Convention should make `agent/*` branches greppable in `git branch -a` so it's obvious which are session-shaped vs human-shaped.

3. **PR-opening helper** — at /goal termination protocol step, the worker runs `gh pr create` with a structured body: bead IDs closed, commits list, test status, briefing-doc reference, experiment-doc reference. (Probably a small shell helper in `~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/bin/goal-pr-create.sh`.)

4. **Goal-protocol skill update** — SKILL.md's "Termination" section currently says "close the bead." Update to: "commit + push branch + open PR + (don't merge)." The bead doesn't close until the PR merges.

5. **Bead lifecycle update** — beads transition through: open → in_progress (worker spawned) → ready-for-review (PR opened) → closed (PR merged). Need to decide if br supports this directly or if we use status=open + a label like `pr:<num>`.

6. **Per-repo override hooks** — some repos may not want this (e.g., this one, where the operator is doing direct main pushes during active development). The per-repo goal-protocol.md can override.

## What stays the same

- Bead-per-commit discipline (per per-repo)
- Test-green at every commit
- Spec amendment in same commit (mu's spec-amend pattern)
- Briefing format (MORNING_<date>.md)

## Out of scope

- **Auto-merge on green CI.** Operator decides each merge. The two-person-control property requires this.
- **Multi-reviewer setup.** Single operator/reviewer for now.
- **Workspace cleanup on PR merge.** Worth doing but later. Manual `jj workspace forget` is acceptable for v1.
- **Migration of past direct-to-main commits.** History is history.

## Trigger to actually pick up

This is the convention shape. Implementing it means:
- Writing the helper scripts
- Updating SKILL.md
- Testing on one /goal session
- Documenting in tool-conventions.md

Should land BEFORE the next autonomous /goal session that isn't operator-watched (the experiments 1-6 yesterday were all operator-watched — the discipline was Thaddeus reviewing each commit in real-time). For background /goal runs (overnight, while operator is away), this is the safety primitive that makes them safe to leave running.

Specifically: if mu-2w7 (TUI design exploration researchers) gets fired and the operator isn't watching, the researchers should work in workspaces + open PRs. NOT push direct-to-main.

## References

- Capability-broker pattern: memory `03b44633` (capability_broker_pattern_thaddeus_systems_2026_05_13)
- CI workflow: `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (mu-jza, landed)
- mu's per-repo goal-protocol overrides: `specs/process/goal-protocol.md`
- Goal-protocol skill: `~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/SKILL.md`

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-14 morning. Conversation context: yesterday's 64-commits-ahead-of-main situation showed how direct-to-main with extended operator-presence works but the alternative (PR flow) is the right standing default. Thaddeus's exact framing: 'Normally I'd go to PR first and then merge, but it's only us right now.' This bead inverts that — PR-first is the default; direct-to-main is the exception, justified per case (e.g. during active live-pair sessions where operator IS the reviewer in real-time).

title: mu-tui-design-exploration: spawn researchers to mock up TUI visual language directions
description:
Filed 2026-05-13 after surveying Charmbracelet's aesthetic (Crush, Glamour, Lip Gloss) + ratatui third-party widgets (throbber, scrollview, tree, term) + bubbletea-rs + oatmeal. Operator note: 'they don't have to work, but it might be interesting to spin off a few different researchers/mockups to get an idea of how we might use some of the visual language in AI terminals with our sensibilities.'

## Premise

The reference shelf (mu-zh1) now has substantial visual-language exemplars. Before committing to specific TUI surface changes (mu-pex / mu-jb3 / mu-ib5 / mu-cha / mu-u8a / mu-di4 / mu-mvk), spin up 2-4 small researcher agents — each takes a *different* design angle — and produce text-mockups + rationale. Pick a direction afterward from concrete artifacts rather than abstract preference.

## Suggested research angles (pick 2-4 to spawn)

1. **Minimal monospaced (helix / atuin aesthetic)** — strict typography, minimal color, no chat-bubble decorations. Information density over delight. Mockup: full mu session display with assistant streaming, tool calls, callouts.

2. **Rich Glamour-styled (Charmbracelet aesthetic)** — termimad-rendered Markdown for assistant responses, lipgloss-equivalent styled borders/panels, throbbers, decorative spacing. Maximal visual affordance. Mockup: same scene as #1 but with full styling.

3. **Operator-dashboard shaped (oatmeal-ish + ratatui multi-pane)** — multi-pane layout: conversation main pane, session-tree side pane (tui-tree-widget), mailbox/callouts pane, status bar with throbbers. For operators managing multiple parallel sessions.

4. **Agent-view-first (an agent's perspective)** — the rope renderer's AgentView form (mu-ovl OperatorView's sibling) made visible. Shows what the model 'sees' alongside what the operator sees. Specifically useful for debugging context-assembly.

Each researcher should produce:
- ASCII mockup of a representative scene (~30-60 lines)
- 5-10 line rationale: what this aesthetic prioritizes, what it sacrifices
- Concrete crate dependencies that would be needed
- Which open beads this aesthetic would naturally complete

## Cost discipline

Each researcher: -3 expected (small scope, mockup + rationale). 2-4 spawns = -12 total. Well under any single /goal experiment.

Use Agent tool with subagent_type=general-purpose or claude. Brief them on the reference shelf (mu-zh1) + relevant open TUI beads. Run them in parallel (single message, multiple Agent tool calls) so the round-trip is one wall-clock.

## Out of scope

Implementation. Mockups only. Pick a direction from the artifacts; THEN file concrete implementation beads against the chosen aesthetic.

## When to fire

- Anytime after 2026-05-13 when operator is fresh and engaged
- Best if mu-pex / mu-jb3 are still open (the aesthetic chosen here would inform their implementation)
- Worse if already locked-in on an aesthetic — at that point we're prototyping, not scoping

## References

Pattern shelf: mu-zh1
Concrete adopt-the-crate beads: mu-di4 (throbber), mu-mvk (termimad markdown)
Strategy beads: mu-yc6 (MCP), mu-f6z (semantic code tool)
Open TUI work: mu-pex (metrics), mu-jb3, mu-ib5 (mailbox), mu-cha (session tree), mu-u8a (color)

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-13 late evening. Operator went to bed. Bead is the durable artifact; the in-conversation context evaporates but this carries the proposal shape forward. When operator returns, deciding 'fire it now' is one explicit message — no re-derivation needed.

title: mu-tui: assistant-block border breaks on wrapped continuation lines
description:
The bordered \"assistant (streaming…)\" block renders its left `│` only on the first line of content. When the text wraps, continuation lines lose the inner-box left border — the wrapped text starts flush against the outer pane's `│`, visually escaping the bounded section.

## Reproduction (2026-05-14)

Opus 4.7 session in mu-tui, right-pane transcript view. Assistant text was long enough to wrap. Observed:

```text
│┌─ assistant (streaming…)                                                          │
││ I'll review the mu workspace systematically. ... examine the agent loop and one  │
│system. Excellent. Let me examine the agent loop and one of the architectural      │
│└─                                                                                 │
```

The continuation line (`system. Excellent...`) is missing the inner `│`. Both top frame `┌─` and bottom frame `└─` render correctly; only the per-line left-edge `│` fails when text wraps.

## Likely cause

In ratatui, the canonical pattern is `Block` (renders the border) + `Paragraph` (wraps inside `Block::inner()`). When the Paragraph wraps text, ratatui's Block + Paragraph composition usually Just Works because the Block draws its borders independently of paragraph content.

If this is broken in mu-tui, likely shapes:
- A custom render path that hand-draws the box's `│` only on the first line of content (forgetting to repeat it per wrapped line);
- A Paragraph rendered into the outer area instead of `Block::inner()`, escaping the block bounds;
- Manual line-splitting that runs before the Block's inner-area-aware wrap;
- A buffer-write that uses absolute column coordinates and doesn't get redrawn for wrapped continuation rows.

## Scope

IN:
- Locate the assistant-block render in `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs` (probably near the transcript-view code).
- Replace any hand-drawn left-edge with the canonical Block+Paragraph composition, OR if already using that pattern, fix the wrap logic so continuation lines inherit the block's left border.
- Visual test: long assistant text should wrap with the inner `│` present on every line.

OUT:
- Markdown rendering (mu-mvk).
- Right-pane phase affordance changes.
- Color polish (mu-u8a).

## Acceptance

- Wrapped assistant text shows the inner `│` on every line of the bordered block.
- No regression on short (single-line) responses.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green

## References

- Observed: 2026-05-14 Opus 4.7 session, screenshot pasted in conversation.
- Render path: `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs` (transcript view; specific line numbers TBD)
- Composes with mu-mvk (markdown rendering) — when mu-mvk lands, the wrapping changes again and this bug may surface differently.

title: mu-036 Phase B: RunMode + handle_start_autonomous + SelfReport loop integration
description:
## Why

Phase A (sister-bead, lands first overnight) adds the wire types, capability extension, and event payloads for mu-036 with no behavioral changes — the dispatch handlers return 'not-yet-implemented' errors. This bead is the next phase: actually wire the autonomous loop.

## What

* Add `RunMode` enum to mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs: Idle | Asking | Autonomous { iteration, goal, options, started_at, tool_calls_consumed } | Sleeping.
* handle_start_autonomous in dispatch.rs: validates session capability includes AutonomyCapability::Allowed, enqueues an AgentInput::StartAutonomous into the session's input channel, returns accepted.
* Agent loop's outer driver: when in Autonomous, the iteration cycle is decide → act → goal-check → decide-next instead of waiting on user inputs. SelfReport goal check first (delegate-grader is Phase D).
* Emit AutonomousIterationStarted / Completed events around each iteration (event payloads land in Phase A).
* Bound enforcement at every iteration boundary: max_iterations, max_wall_clock_ms, max_total_tool_calls_in_autonomy (from the session's Capability, NOT the request options — INV-2).
* Tests: scripted Autonomous over FauxProvider; iteration count exactly hits the bound; SelfReport goal=satisfied terminates early.

## Out of scope (separate beads)

* Phase C: schedule_wakeup + Sleeping state
* Phase D: DelegateGrader + ExternalSignal goal check methods
* Escalate-on-idle (INV-4 mention)
* TUI surface for autonomous sessions

## Acceptance

Workspace tests green; new tests cover the iteration cycle + bound enforcement against FauxProvider; AutonomousTerminated is always the final event (INV-7).

title: Future: secret-aware prompt/content scanning and redaction
description:
Add pre-send/pre-log secret scanning and optional content decoupling. Baseline should be default-on, configurable mechanical local scanners (rg/regex/entropy/prefix) for API keys, PEM blocks, JWTs, provider tokens, .env-style assignments, high-entropy strings, etc.

Events may store content_sha256/content_ref/redaction_status/redacted_preview rather than full text when appropriate; plaintext can live in a local/encrypted/optional content store.

Optional later layer: fast local-only LLM/classifier for semantic sensitivity (HIPAA, classified markings, corporate IP), advisory/risk-scoring only — not the sole guard. Remote models must never be used to detect secrets before sending, because that is already exfiltration.

Related memories: bc1d46ec, ac66f02f.

title: session.set_provider RPC: mid-session model swap
description:
## Why

Companion to mu-j1b (per-ask provider override). That bead is for one-shot overrides; this one is for the *persistent* swap: 'from now on, this session uses sonnet'. Use case: a session that started cheap (haiku) and now needs more capability — current workaround is close+create a fresh session, losing the in-memory context (assistant messages, tool result history).

## What

New RPC: `session.set_provider`. Wire:

```json
{
  "session_id": "session-1",
  "provider": { "kind": "anthropic_api", "model": "claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929" }
}
```

Response: `{ "swapped": true, "from": {...}, "to": {...} }` or an error if validation fails.

Mutates the session's stored ProviderSelector. Subsequent asks (with no override) use the new provider.

## Considerations

- **Conversation context portability**: the existing AgentMessage history is provider-agnostic (it's mu's internal types, not provider-specific format). So swapping should JUST WORK from a state perspective — the next translate_messages call will render the same history into the new provider's wire shape.
- **Tool compatibility**: the new provider must support the session's currently-bound tools. Validate at swap time and fail with an explanatory error rather than at next ask time.
- **Cache invalidation**: like mu-j1b, the new provider has no cache hit on the old prefix. Document this in the response so callers can decide whether to swap mid-session.
- **Should this emit a SessionCreated-style event?** It's a state change worth a durable record. Probably yes — a new EventPayload::ProviderSwapped { from, to, at_event_id } variant. mu-pex's aggregator would need to be updated to handle the case where a session's (provider, model) changes mid-stream: maybe split the session into two virtual sessions in usage_history rows. Phase-2 detail.

## Acceptance

- Swap succeeds; subsequent asks use the new provider.
- Session's event log records the swap.
- Validation error when the new provider can't serve the session's tools.

## Cross-references

- mu-j1b — per-ask provider override (this bead's complement)
- mu-pex Phase 2 — aggregator behavior under mid-session provider swap

title: kqueue/inotify watches on file-loaded spans, with signal/policy separation
description:
When a span is loaded from a file (skill, tool schema, system prompt template, CLAUDE.md), register an OS file watch. On change, emit a structured event. Policy on the event is separate from the signal.

## Mechanism

FreeBSD: kqueue (EVFILT_VNODE, NOTE_WRITE|DELETE|RENAME). Linux: inotify (IN_MODIFY|DELETE|MOVE). macOS: kqueue or FSEvents. Cross-platform via notify crate or cfg-gated.

Emits SourceChangedEvent { span_id, source_path, change_kind, detected_at }.

## Policies (separate from signal)

Default: don't auto-reload. Rope addresses version-at-activation-time. Preserves reproducibility — 'what did the model know at turn N?' answerable consistently before and after a file change.

Explicit: rope.rehydrate(span_id) reloads and emits span.rehydrated event.

Configurable (deferred follow-up): auto_rehydrate_on_change false/true; auto_rehydrate_for_kinds list.

## Scope

In: notify crate (verify FreeBSD support before commit); SourceChangedEvent emission; RetainedRope::rehydrate API; tests (temp dir + touch); doc section in event-sourced-context.md.

Out: per-session policy config (follow-up bead); TUI stale-span indicator (operator-view enhancement); directory watches for skill-with-references (needs design pass).

## Why

Reproducibility by default; reload is explicit and audited; multiple policies coexist (dev auto-rehydrates skills; prod never); watch is cheap regardless of policy.

## Acceptance

File watches register on span load; modifying a watched file emits SourceChangedEvent within <1s latency; default policy: span unchanged after event (only rehydrate updates); rehydrate emits separate event; tested at minimum on FreeBSD (kqueue); workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-6-file-watch-source-changes.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8, mu-nat
Memory: 3cc7a18c

title: mu-loop-guard-finally-hang: loop-guard.py's finally cleanup hangs after result-event break instead of exiting in 10s
description:
Follow-on bug uncovered by experiment 6 (mu-cap-aws, 2026-05-13). Reopened post-experiments 7a/7b. Reproduced 2026-05-14 in experiment 7c (mu-nat).

## Observed behavior

After loop-guard sees the `result` event, the new break-on-result-event logic fires correctly (`[loop-guard] saw result event; exiting cleanly` IS the last line in the session log). But the wrapping bash process then hangs indefinitely (in experiment 7c: ~30 min past actual work completion before operator-issued SIGTERM resolved it cleanly with exit code 0).

This means loop-guard's `finally` cleanup (proc.stdout.close + proc.wait timeout=10 + killpg escalation) is NOT exiting in the expected ≤15s window.

## Experiment 7c evidence (2026-05-14)

- Worker emitted `RESULT subtype=success cost_usd=6.199858249999999 turns=63` at T+~3min after start.
- Wrapper STILL alive at T+17:45min.
- Inspected process tree: claude-code subprocess GONE (no children of loop-guard PID match claude-code); ONLY child of loop-guard was an ssh-agent process (PID 9020, PGID 9020, became its own process-group leader).
- External SIGTERM to loop-guard PID exited cleanly (exit code 0). No SIGKILL escalation needed from outside.
- The ssh-agent child is suspicious — ssh-agent normally daemonizes; if claude-code's environment triggered an ssh-agent spawn that didn't fully daemonize, ssh-agent could hold the pipe handle open and keep proc.stdout.close()/proc.wait() blocked.

## Refined hypotheses

1. **ssh-agent grandchild orphan**: claude-code or one of its hooks/extensions invokes ssh-agent (perhaps via SSH_AUTH_SOCK / git operations). If ssh-agent fails to fully daemonize (doesn't close inherited fds), it keeps the pipe alive past claude-code's exit. proc.wait blocks waiting for the pipe to close.
2. **Python pipe-iterator cleanup race**: `for line in proc.stdout:` followed by `break` may leave the iterator in a state where proc.stdout.close() blocks if any descendant still has the write side open.
3. **proc.wait() / SIGCHLD race**: Python's wait may miss the child-death notification under start_new_session=True; subsequent wait calls block waiting for a SIGCHLD that already fired.

## Investigation steps

1. **Test hypothesis 1**: in loop-guard, after the break, explicitly close stderr too and call os.killpg(proc.pid, SIGKILL) IMMEDIATELY (not after a 10s wait). If wrapper still hangs, ssh-agent is keeping the pipe open. If it exits cleanly, the original finally's 10s wait was the slow part.
2. **Test hypothesis 2**: replace `for line in proc.stdout:` with explicit `proc.stdout.readline()` calls in a `while True:` loop. Detach iterator semantics.
3. **Add safety net**: wrap the whole finally in an alarm-signal-based timeout (signal.SIGALRM after 30s). If finally hasn't completed, hard exit via os._exit(2).

## Acceptance

- Loop-guard exits within 15s of seeing the result event when claude-code completes normally.
- Loop-guard exits within 15s of SIGTERM-ing claude-code when loop-detection fires.
- Synthetic test (subprocess that emits 'result' then sleep(60)) AND real claude-code test BOTH terminate the wrapper in ≤15s.
- Diagnostic logging captures finally-block timing so future regressions are visible.

## References

- Current loop-guard: ~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/bin/loop-guard.py
- Original synthetic test: passed
- Real-claude-code reproductions: experiments 7a, 7b (2026-05-14 morning), 7c (2026-05-14 afternoon)
- The 30-min hang in 7c was cheap (cost-wise) because work was complete; only wasted a process slot. But it blocks operator's ability to know "the worker is truly done" without inspecting the session JSONL directly.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Reopened: Reopening with new diagnostic evidence: the earlier fix (start_new_session=True + os.killpg SIGTERM/SIGKILL escalation in finally) PASSED a synthetic test (result-event-then-sleep(60) script) but FAILED against real claude-code behavior in experiments 7a (mu-di4 throbber, bash bjmrxdrw0) and 7b (mu-bn4 Anthropic, bash bakpyuacy) on 2026-05-14. Both /goal sessions completed their work cleanly (~9 min each, result events emitted, PRs opened) but the wrapper processes hung until the 5400s wall-clock timeout fired (~80 min after actual completion). [loop-guard] 'saw result event; exiting cleanly' line IS present in both session logs, confirming the break-on-result-event detection fires correctly. The hang is somewhere in the finally cleanup — proc.wait(timeout=10) followed by os.killpg(SIGTERM/SIGKILL) is not actually killing what's holding the subprocess tree alive. New hypotheses: (1) claude-code spawns processes that detach via their own setsid call, escaping our process group; (2) Python subprocess.Popen's stdout pipe-handle keeps a reference alive even after kill; (3) some specific child (auto-memory hook, attribution sender) catches SIGTERM but not SIGKILL, and SIGKILL isn't being propagated to grandchildren. Investigation should: (a) reproduce with a synthetic that spawns its own setsid-detached children, (b) inspect /proc/<pid>/status or ps -o for child process tree at 30s post-result-event in a real claude-code run, (c) try killpg with -9 (SIGKILL) directly without the SIGTERM-first step. Synthetic-was-insufficient is the meta-lesson; real claude-code behavior on shutdown is more complex than a simple sleep loop.

title: TUI: system_prompt palette commands + Ctrl-X Ctrl-P $EDITOR chord
description:
## Why

mu-n48 (commit xollnzmv 2026-05-11/12) threads system_prompt through Provider::stream and surfaces it in the wire protocol. But the TUI doesn't expose it: pressing 'n' always sends `CreateSessionRequest { provider, system_prompt: None }`. Closing this gap completes mu-n48's user-visible story.

## What

Two complementary affordances:

### 1. Palette commands

```
:system_prompt                  → show current default in firehose
:system_prompt <one-line text>  → set as default for next 'n'
:clear_system_prompt            → unset
```

Stored as `App.default_system_prompt: Option<String>` alongside the existing `default_provider` tuple.

### 2. Ctrl-X Ctrl-P chord ('P' for Prompt)

In SendPrompt mode (after pressing 'i'), pressing Ctrl-X then Ctrl-P bounces the **current default system_prompt** into $EDITOR — same handoff machinery as mu-82l's Ctrl-X Ctrl-E for the user prompt. Empty buffer if no current default; on editor save, store as the new default. Trailing \n stripped.

Most system prompts are multi-paragraph and benefit from a real editor; one-line palette form is for quick toggles.

### 3. Header display

When a default is set, header shows `sys: ✓` (with content as a tooltip via :system_prompt to display, not inline — system prompts are too long to dump inline).

## Wiring

`create_session` JSON now includes system_prompt when set:

```json
{
  "provider": { "kind": "...", "model": "..." },
  "system_prompt": "..."
}
```

The wire field has been accepted since mu-n48; this just populates it from the TUI.

## Acceptance

- :system_prompt 'be concise' sets the prompt; next 'n' creates a session that respects it.
- Ctrl-X Ctrl-P opens $EDITOR with current default, save updates it, :cq leaves unchanged.
- :clear_system_prompt removes the default; next 'n' sends `system_prompt: None`.
- F5 cache% column rises on multi-ask sessions when a meaningful system prompt is set (the live-verify already showed +~2.5% cache hit rate for an 80-token prompt).

## Cross-references

- mu-n48: provider wiring
- mu-82l: $EDITOR chord pattern (reuse `open_in_editor`)
- mu-i6j: cache_control on tools; the system prompt joins it as cached prefix

title: TUI: $EDITOR integration for prompt editing (Ctrl-X Ctrl-E)
description:
## Why

For prompts longer than a few lines, in-place editing in the TUI is limiting even after mu-wd2 (basic readline). zsh's edit-command-line binding (`Ctrl-X Ctrl-E`) is the canonical pattern for 'this is too long to type comfortably here; let me bounce into a real editor'.

Discussed 2026-05-11. Helix-core embedding was considered and rejected — $EDITOR delivers the same value (you get any editor, including Helix when $EDITOR=hx) at a fraction of the implementation cost.

## How the pattern works

The TUI's state lives in-memory (App struct in Rust). The terminal display is the *output* of that state, not the storage. So the editor handoff is just 'who currently owns the terminal':

1. Persist current prompt buffer to a tempfile (with a .md suffix so editors syntax-highlight markdown by default).
2. Tear down our terminal grip: `disable_raw_mode()`, `execute!(stdout(), LeaveAlternateScreen)`. The shell's prior scrollback comes back.
3. Spawn $EDITOR (default 'vi' if unset) on the tempfile. It inherits stdin/stdout/stderr from us; it owns the terminal.
4. Block on the editor's status (synchronous; UX-wise, the TUI 'pauses' while the editor is open).
5. On editor exit: `execute!(stdout(), EnterAlternateScreen)`, `enable_raw_mode()`. We're back in a fresh alt-screen canvas; next render tick repaints the App.
6. Read the tempfile back, populate the prompt buffer.
7. Clean up the tempfile.

Total: ~30 LOC plus a key-binding entry.

## Keybinding

Ctrl-X Ctrl-E (matching zsh / bash readline). Or, if Ctrl-X is reserved for something else in the TUI, F12, or :edit palette command.

## Edge cases

- Editor exits with non-zero status (user did :cq in vi to abort): treat as cancel — don't update the prompt buffer.
- Tempfile cleanup on panic: use a RAII guard (`tempfile::NamedTempFile` does this).
- Slow editors / large files: not a real concern for prompts; mu's prompts are typically <10KB. If this ever matters, async/spawn-thread variants exist.

## Acceptance

- Pressing Ctrl-X Ctrl-E from input mode opens $EDITOR with current prompt content.
- Saving + exiting populates the prompt buffer with the edited content.
- Cancelling (non-zero exit) leaves the prompt buffer unchanged.
- Works with vi, nvim, hx, nano, ed in CI tests (or at minimum, a faux-editor that just echoes 'X' into the file).

## Cross-reference

- mu-wd2 (arrow keys in-place) — complement, not replacement. Most short prompts will still be edited in-place; $EDITOR is for the long-prompt case.

title: Session templates: startup-time spawn presets for common (provider, model) combos
description:
## Why

Filed 2026-05-12 from the design discussion after mu-ium landed.

User workflow surfacing: typical daily work involves a small recurring set of (provider, model) sessions — e.g. opus-4-7 for hard reasoning, haiku-4-5 for cheap subtasks, gpt-5.5 for codex routing. Today, opening these requires 3+ rounds of `:provider` / `:model` / `n` per session. A 'session template' shortcut would let the user pick a named preset that spawns all the sessions in one keystroke (or auto-spawns on launch when a default is configured).

This is the multi-session generalization of mu-ium (which made one-at-a-time provider selection runtime-controllable).

## What

Two reasonable mechanisms, complementary not exclusive:

### A. Interactive startup picker

When mu-tui launches with no auto-template configured, show a small picker overlay:

```
  Choose a session template:
  ▶ daily       (3 sessions: opus, haiku, codex)
    review      (2 sessions: 2× sonnet)
    cheap       (1 session: haiku)
    [skip]      Start with no sessions
```

Selection spawns all the sessions in the template; user lands in F1 with them already created.

### B. Config-driven autostart

Templates live in `~/.config/mu/templates.toml` (or similar):

```toml
[templates.daily]
sessions = [
    { provider = "anthropic_api", model = "claude-opus-4-7",     name = "opus" },
    { provider = "anthropic_api", model = "claude-haiku-4-5...", name = "haiku" },
    { provider = "openai_codex",  model = "gpt-5.5",             name = "codex" },
]

[startup]
default_template = "daily"   # auto-spawn on launch
# default_template = ""        # show picker instead
```

A CLI flag overrides: `mu-tui --template review` (and a corresponding `--no-auto` or `--template -` to skip).

### Optional session naming

Templates can name their sessions (the `name` field above). When set, the session list (F1 / F3 picker) shows the name alongside the session_id — much friendlier than 'session-1, session-2, session-3' when you're juggling 3+.

## Cross-references

- mu-ium (closed): runtime :provider / :model commands. This bead extends to multi-session, presets.
- mu-cha (open, P2): F2 session tree. The picker variant could be folded into F2's session-management surface.
- mu-h04 / mu-wd2 (closed): TUI input ergonomics. Same author-of-frustration ('I do this every day, why can't it remember').

## Acceptance

- mu-tui launches with `--template daily` and creates the sessions listed in that template.
- If no template flag and config has `default_template`, that template auto-spawns.
- If neither, an interactive picker appears (or, if config disables it, the existing 'empty F1' state).
- Optional names for sessions are honored when set.
- Bad template (missing API key for a referenced provider) gracefully skips that session with a firehose warning, doesn't abort the whole template.

title: mu-web-fetch: web/HTTP tool surface (consolidate curl + yt-dlp + pdf converters into MCP)
description:
Today, agents fetch web content via shelling out to `curl` / `yt-dlp` / `pdftotext` from the Bash tool. This works but: (1) every model invocation rebuilds the right invocation, (2) Bash's broad surface means web access has the same blast radius as arbitrary code execution, (3) no structured output (URL, content-type, status, body — all as text from stdout).

Operator preference (2026-05-14): consolidate into an MCP tool server, not built-ins. Control gain: the MCP gets its own attenuated capability (web.fetch.readonly, web.download.bucket, etc.), distinct from bash-everywhere.

## Scope

IN:
- Stdio MCP server exposing structured tools: `web_fetch(url, headers?)`, `youtube_transcript(url)`, `pdf_extract(path_or_url)`, possibly `html_to_md(url)`. Underlying impls wrap curl / yt-dlp / pdftotext / pandoc as today, but the AGENT sees typed tool calls.
- Capability namespace: `web.fetch.readonly` (any URL), `web.download.<scoped>` (e.g. specific domain prefix) — modeled on the AWS capability namespace pattern (mu-f5o struct-of-axes).
- Output: structured (status, content-type, body or extracted text).
- Implemented as MCP so it can be reused by other agents/harnesses, not just mu.

OUT:
- Built-in Rust HTTP client. The MCP server can be in any language; the tool registration shape is the wire surface.
- Full browser automation (Selenium/Playwright). v1 is read-only fetch + extract.
- Streaming responses (server-sent events from upstream). v1 collects then returns.

## Acceptance

- MCP server binary or script that mu can register via the MCP support landed in mu-yc6 (blocked-by)
- At least three tool methods working: fetch, youtube transcript, pdf extract
- Each tool's capability surface is documented in the server's tool schema
- Integration test: a mu session can read a URL via the MCP, content arrives as structured output

## Dependencies

- **Blocked by mu-yc6** (MCP plugin/tool-server support) — until mu has an MCP client, this server has nowhere to register.
- Capability namespace design (struct-of-axes from mu-f5o gives the shape; need to add a `web` axis or fold into a more general `network` axis).

## References

- mu-yc6: MCP support in mu-coding
- mu-f5o: capability struct-of-axes pattern; this web axis mirrors the AWS axis shape
- Operator note 2026-05-14: 'Right now webfetch is curl, yt-something to process youtube, and pdfto* programs. Certainly can consolidate into an MCP to help with control rather than having to use bash.'

title: Implement mu-038 FileBackend — cross-daemon same-machine discovery
description:
Spec at specs/mu-038-projection-queries-and-discovery.md (jj qrpzxpuq 1af4f26a).

FileBackend writes TOML files under ~/.local/share/mu/daemons/<daemon_id>/sessions/<session_id>.toml on announce, deletes on withdraw. session.list with include_remote=true enumerates files across all daemon directories.

Liveness via daemon.heartbeat file mtime — daemons touch it every N seconds. Readers treat sessions from a daemon with stale heartbeat (>30s) as 'may be dead'.

Cross-daemon trust is OUT of scope here — same-user-on-same-machine assumed. Auth comes later (likely biscuit-auth).

title: Future: readonly / inspection mode for mutating tools
description:
Add a readonly/inspection run mode. Agents may perform read-only work autonomously, but write/edit/bash/network/delegate/mutating actions pause at the exact proposed tool call for human inspection.

Approval UI should show session/task ownership, proposed args, paths/commands, diff/impact preview where available, and choices: allow once, allow scope/pattern for this session, deny call, cancel tool, cancel session/delegate, or quiesce all.

Composes with session.input_required, tool policy, and emergency cancel-only mode.

Related memory: 73505005.

title: Future: emergency stop / cancel-only control plane
description:
Design and implement explicit operator controls for runaway/unsafe agent work.

Modes to support eventually:
- normal
- readonly / inspection
- cancel-only / quiesce: no new model calls, tool calls, or delegates; only reduce exposure / summarize / stop
- abort / evict all: cancel active tool calls, stop delegates, terminate subprocess groups/jails
- hard stop / recovery: immediate kill with explicit reconciliation on restart

Dangerous broad actions should use guarded-trigger UX: show affected sessions/tools/delegates, require typed nonce confirmation, optionally reason/operator, and emit durable audit events. Inspired by industrial emergency-stop controls and matching-engine cancel-only / order-eviction controls.

Related memories: 1440515e, 516fedc7, 0d326bed, c8473c42.

title: AnthropicProviderRenderer + AnthropicCacheStrategy (mu-ktq follow-on)
description:
Follow-on to mu-ktq (foundational traits, closed). Provide the Anthropic-specific implementations of the new traits introduced in crates/mu-core/src/context/.

## Scope

In:
- `AnthropicProviderRenderer` impl of `ProviderRenderer` — render a RetainedRope into Anthropic-shaped Messages API request payload (system prompt + messages array, content blocks). Honor `ProjectionTarget::AgentView` (full fidelity for the model); operator-view rendering can be added later.
- `AnthropicCacheStrategy` impl of `CacheStrategy` — place a single cache boundary at the last stable+cacheable span position (per spec lines 567-578); annotate that message with `CacheMarker::Ephemeral`; the Anthropic adapter translates that into `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral" }` at the boundary message.
- Tests: at least one rope shape with a clear stable/volatile split; verify boundary placement; verify annotation lands on the right message.

Out:
- Adoption in the live agent loop (separate bead — see "live-loop adoption").
- OpenAI-specific rendering (separate bead).
- Full RetainedRope (= mu-nat).

## Design questions to resolve before implementing

1. Coexistence with existing mu-i6j cache_control work — where does the current `cache_control` annotation get applied today? Does the new strategy replace that code path, or does it sit alongside until the adoption bead lands?
2. The current Anthropic adapter takes `&[AgentMessage]` (mu-core/src/agent/provider.rs:62). Does the new renderer return ProviderMessages and a separate adapter step converts to the Anthropic wire JSON, or should AnthropicProviderRenderer go all the way to the wire shape?
3. System-prompt placement: Anthropic has a top-level `system` field (mu-n48), distinct from messages. Where does the system-role span fall in the rendered output?

## Acceptance

Trait impls compile and link against existing mu-ai Anthropic code paths; tests verify boundary placement; cargo test --workspace green; bead-close commit with file:line citations.

## References

- mu-ktq (closed; foundational traits)
- specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md:564-612
- mu-i6j (existing cache_control work)
- mu-n48 (system prompt support)
close_reason: Landed in PR #4, commit 98e174f (squash-merged from agent/bn4-anthropic-2026-05-14). AnthropicProviderRenderer + AnthropicCacheStrategy in crates/mu-ai/src/context/anthropic.rs. Design questions resolved in commit body + module docs: sit alongside mu-i6j; ProviderMessages return type; System-kind → System role (future adapter hoists to top-level system field). 13 new tests + 156+ workspace green. Unblocks mu-fb0 (live-loop adoption).

title: BUG / external: openai-codex backend stalls — track for awareness
description:
On 2026-05-11 (overnight + into morning) the openai-codex Responses API became unresponsive — >100s with zero wire signal for trivial prompts ('reply with READY').

Token fresh (expires 2026-05-20). Defensive layer surfaces it as MuClientTimeout. Anthropic API works fine in parallel — confirms provider-specific.

Not our bug — but track here so we have a place to note when it recurs / resolves, and so any 'why is codex hung again' question has a single answer.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Status update 2026-05-11 evening: user has been successfully completing both openai-codex and anthropic-api calls in the past few hours; the 14hr stall observed 2026-05-10/11 seems to have resolved vendor-side. Not closing — stalls in cloud-API layers can recur intermittently and the right thing is to keep watching.

mu-035 Phase B (periodic heartbeat) was designed specifically to prevent silent hangs like the one that motivated this bead — if/when a stall recurs, the heartbeat ticks should make it visible in F8 firehose almost immediately, and session.cancel_outstanding (mu-035 Phase C) provides a clean abort path. Cross-filed as a new monitoring task to keep codex in regular rotation so we'd notice a recurrence within a day rather than weeks.

title: Cluster-service spec (mu-040): cross-machine federation
description:
Per memory d32898bd. Cross-machine mu visibility goes through a SEPARATE cluster-service process, not via etcd-backed-into-each-daemon.

Service responsibilities:
- Accept client connections (TUI, web UI, agents)
- Authentication / authorization
- Use etcd to enumerate sessions across all daemons in the cluster
- Aggregate cross-daemon mailbox routing

Daemons stay narrow: they just announce themselves + sessions to etcd via a thin SessionDiscovery::EtcdAnnouncerBackend (or similar). The aggregation happens elsewhere.

Future spec — write when we have a real driver for cross-machine work. Likely paired with biscuit-auth for the trust layer.

title: TUI: pagination + scrolling for long transcripts

title: mu-tui-throbber: render ProviderStatusSnapshot as throbber+label via throbber-widgets-tui
description:
Adopt the `throbber-widgets-tui` crate (https://github.com/arkbig/throbber-widgets-tui) and render mu-035's `ProviderStatusSnapshot` states as an animated throbber + label, replacing the current static state-string render.

## Why

Today, the TUI renders provider state as text — `awaiting_first_token` / `streaming` / etc. with elapsed_ms. A throbber widget gives visual feedback that the agent is alive and working (the same role the spinner serves in IDE tooling). Concrete and small.

## Scope

IN:
- Add `throbber-widgets-tui` to mu-tui's Cargo.toml deps

- Render in the phase line area where `state + elapsed_ms` currently lives (around main.rs:130+; the snapshot type was annotated in mu-lm8)
- Animation steps + symbol set: pick from the crate's built-ins; default looks fine for v1

OUT:
- Throbber per individual tool call (could come later — mu-pex / mu-jb3 might want this)
- Custom symbol sets / theming (mu-u8a is the theming track)

## Acceptance

- ProviderStatusSnapshot renders with an animated throbber alongside the state string
- Throbber animation ticks reliably (no stuck frames)

title: mu-ovl-B: Implement OperatorView rendering rules per SpanKind
description:
Sub-bead of mu-ovl. Behavior phase. Replaces the shallow OperatorView prefix-tag at `renderer.rs:224` with per-SpanKind rendering rules that make AgentView and OperatorView visibly different.

## Scope

IN: in `FauxProviderRenderer::render_content` (`renderer.rs:221+`), match on `SpanKind` and produce distinct OperatorView output for each variant. Suggested rules (autonomous session may refine — record final choices in commit body):

| SpanKind | AgentView | OperatorView |
|---|---|---|
| System / User / Assistant | full content | full content |
| ToolResult | full content (raw JSON or text) | one-line structured summary — e.g., `[tool-result: ok, 3 items]` or first N chars |
| ToolCall | full content (name + args JSON) | name + arg keys only — e.g., `call: bash(command)` |
| ToolSchema | full schema JSON | name only — e.g., `[tool: bash]` |
| SkillActivation | full activation event | `[skill: <name>]` badge |
| MemoryInjection | full memory content | `<memory id=X collapsed>...</memory>` collapsible tag |
| Compaction | full synthetic span content | `[compacted N spans: <summary>]` |
| FileLoad | full file content | `[file: <path>] <N lines>` |

Add a `SpanKindMetadata` or inline policy table in renderer.rs documenting the rules.

Tests: hand-build a rope containing at least one span of each new kind, render under both targets, assert different output structures. The 'same input, two outputs' contrast is the load-bearing demonstration of the projection abstraction.

OUT: TUI integration (consuming OperatorView output in mu-pex / mu-jb3). File a follow-on bead `mu-ovl-tui` blocked-by mu-ovl-B for that work.

## Acceptance

- Per-SpanKind rule landed for all new variants
- Test asserting AgentView != OperatorView output for at least 4 variants
- Test asserting AgentView output is unchanged for the 4 original variants
- `cargo test --workspace` green at commit
- Commit body documents the final per-variant rule choices (if they diverged from this table)
- Follow-on bead `mu-ovl-tui` filed for TUI consumption

## References

title: mu-cap-aws: Capability gains AWS-namespace axis + intersect()
description:
Add an AWS-capability axis to the existing `Capability` struct in `crates/mu-core/src/capability.rs`, with intersect() preserving the narrowing-only invariant.

This is the 'mu is ready' gate for the AWS integration described in `~/src/personal/mu-aws-sandbox-infra/docs/mu-integration.md` and the capability-broker pattern (memory `03b44633` — capability_broker_pattern_thaddeus_systems_2026_05_13).

## Scope

IN:
- New `AwsCapability` type (struct with `name: String` and `session_policy: Option<serde_json::Value>`). `name` matches the catalog at `~/src/personal/mu-aws-sandbox-infra/capabilities/aws.json` (e.g., `aws.scout.readonly`). `session_policy` field type-prepares the biscuit-shaped narrowing axis without committing to its use yet.
- Add `aws: Option<AwsCapability>` (or `aws: HashSet<AwsCapability>` — worker's call; see Design questions) to the `Capability` struct.
- Extend `Capability::intersect()` to include the AWS axis with narrowing-only semantics.
- Spec amendment in `specs/architecture/capability-delegation.md` describing the new axis. Folds into the same commit per per-repo 'spec-amend commit' pattern.
- Tests: same-name same/none session-policy passes; different-name behavior matches the worker's chosen semantics; expiry/tools/autonomy axes unchanged by AWS axis addition.

OUT:
- The capability-broker / judge-orchestrator runtime — separate beads, blocked-by mu-037 (peer.hello + mailbox).
- An `aws-recon` skill that actually USES this capability — separate bead.
- Cross-daemon biscuit serialization (the spec calls out future biscuit-auth swap; that's still future).
- General-purpose Capability::Foo enum refactor — the existing struct-of-axes shape is already more idiomatic for this pattern.

## Design questions (worker decides; record choices in commit body)

1. **Single `aws: Option<AwsCapability>` vs `aws: HashSet<AwsCapability>`.** A worker may legitimately hold multiple AWS capabilities at once (scout + s3-write). HashSet supports that; Option does not. HashSet is more flexible but requires hashable AwsCapability. Lean: HashSet, but worker may justify Option if it lands first.
2. **Different-name intersect semantics.** If parent has scout.readonly and child requests sandbox.build, what does intersect produce? Options: empty set (HashSet model), intersect-on-name-match-only (subset semantics), or None (refuse incompatible). Pick semantics that preserve narrowing-only.
3. **session_policy axis.** Carried but unused for v1. intersect() between two Some session_policies: leave deferred (worker may halt at this; spec the deferral).

## Acceptance

- New AwsCapability struct (or chosen shape) defined; `Capability` struct gains `aws` field
- intersect() updated to include AWS axis; narrowing-only invariant holds
- Spec amended in same commit; cite chosen shape rationale at the spec line that introduces the new axis
- Tests: serde round-trip for AwsCapability; intersect cases (same name, multiple names if HashSet, missing axis on either side, expiry interaction unchanged)
- cargo test --workspace green at commit
- One commit closes this bead

## References

Memory: `03b44633` (capability-broker pattern), `8b46733d`/`dac304e2` (Edit-needs-Read + loop-guard lessons).
Doc: `~/src/personal/mu-aws-sandbox-infra/docs/mu-integration.md` (esp. 'Proposed shape: typed Capability enum' section — note the existing code is struct-of-axes, not enum-of-variants; worker should integrate accordingly).
Code: `crates/mu-core/src/capability.rs` (existing Capability struct + intersect at line 132+); `AutonomyCapability` is the model for adding a new axis cleanly.
Spec: `specs/architecture/capability-delegation.md` (current shape + invariants).
close_reason: Landed in commit 73741606. AwsCapability struct + aws: HashSet axis on Capability + symmetric Capability::intersect() + spec amendment in one commit per spec-amend pattern. 13 new tests; INV-1 narrowing-only assertion across all 5 axes (including AWS) passing. session_policy intersect deferred. cargo test --workspace green (360 tests).

title: mu-semantic-code-tool: agent tool for semantic code lookup (treesitter and/or LSP/rust-analyzer)
description:
Placeholder for a semantic code-understanding tool surface available to agents (alongside the existing read/grep/glob/edit/bash/write primitives). Motivation: spawns currently see code as tokens via grep; want them to see structure (functions, scopes, definitions, callers) when that matters.

## Two possible implementations

**Treesitter (syntactic):**
- Fast, language-agnostic, stable parser output
- Good for: "find all functions matching pattern X", "find all impl blocks for trait Y"
- Limits: can't resolve identifiers — two references to `foo` look the same even if they bind to different definitions
- Cost: pick a Rust binding (tree-sitter-rust crate), define a query DSL surface for agents, wire as new tool in crates/mu-coding/src/tools/

**LSP / rust-analyzer (semantic):**
- Slower to start, real semantic analysis (scope, types, definitions, references)
- Good for: "find the definition of this symbol", "find all usages of this function"
- Limits: Rust-only; one daemon per workspace; cold-start cost (~10s warm-up)
- Cost: integrate with rust-analyzer's LSP server, or a slim subset (textDocument/definition, textDocument/references)

The two are complementary, not exclusive. Different jobs:
- Treesitter: "find this pattern in code" (syntactic, language-agnostic)
- LSP: "find the definition of this symbol" (semantic, language-specific)

## Trigger conditions for actually building this (defer until at least one fires)

1. An autonomous /goal run silently misses something structural with grep and burns budget down the wrong path (concrete failure attributable to grep's limits, not the worker's regex error).
2. Multi-language work enters mu's scope (treesitter's superpower is cross-language uniformity; until then rust-analyzer covers Rust better).
3. A refactoring pattern recurs enough across beads to be worth a tool (e.g. repeated 'find all callers of this fn' surveys; today's mu-j97 was the first hint).

## Out of scope

- Building either implementation now. This bead is a placeholder + design notes for when one of the triggers fires.
- AST-aware Edit primitives. Possible follow-on once a base structural tool exists.
- Tree-sitter for non-Rust languages (mu-coding's prompt-engineering primitives ARE multi-language but the current call surface assumes file content).

## Acceptance (when this is actually picked up)

- Choose one of the two implementations (or both, as separate tools)
- Wire as a new `crate::tools::*` module in mu-coding with the existing Tool trait shape
- Define the query/output surface that agents see (string-in, structured-out)
- Tests + spec doc following the per-repo convention

## Notes

- Motivation discussed 2026-05-13: Thaddeus wants ways to help spawns 'look at things semantically rather than just a bunch of tokens.' Not urgent; filing while it's named.
- Compare cost/benefit: see also mu-pex (metrics framework) — both are agent-tooling-shaped beads; ordering depends on which agent-failure mode bites first.
- Related: existing mu-coding tools live in crates/mu-coding/src/tools/{bash,edit,grep,read,write,glob}.rs — the integration point is straightforward.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Scope expansion noted 2026-05-13 after surveying Crush** (https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush). Crush lists 'LSP-Enhanced: uses LSPs for additional context, just like you do' as a first-class feature. This is a STRONGER framing than the 'agent-queryable structural tool' shape I originally captured.

Two distinct primitives in play:

1. **LSP / treesitter as an agent-queryable TOOL** — agent asks 'find the definition of X', gets back file:line + snippet. Pull model. (Original bead scope.)

2. **LSP / treesitter as a CONTEXT-ENRICHMENT layer** — when the agent loop assembles context for a model call, automatically include relevant definitions, type signatures, or callers based on what the conversation references. Push model. (Crush's framing.)

The push model is more powerful but bigger: it requires the agent loop (or a context-assembly layer in mu-coding) to know what's worth enriching from the current conversation. That's a substantial architectural addition beyond a simple Tool trait impl.

When picked up, decide which (or both) primitives mu wants. The pull model is easier to add incrementally; the push model is what makes 'agents that see semantic code' really shine.

title: Adopt ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy in live agent loop (mu-ktq follow-on)
description:

title: TUI: short prompt gets obscured by overlapping UI element
description:
## Why

Reported by user, 2026-05-11: "when typing a prompt over a short sentence, it gets obscured by the tooltip."

The prompt input field overlaps with another rendered element (the user said "tooltip" — could be the F-key tab row, the firehose, the statusline, or an overlay). Net effect: typed text becomes hard to read while in input mode.

## What I don't know yet

- Which element is doing the overlapping (need a screenshot or terminal-size repro).
- At what terminal sizes it manifests (probably narrow heights — the firehose / statusline competing with the input area for the same rows).
- Whether the overlap is z-order (one widget drawing over another) or layout (Constraints summing to more than the available area).

## What

After repro:
- If z-order: re-order the render calls so the input field is the last thing drawn.
- If layout: the Layout::default().constraints([...]) somewhere is summing to too much for short terminals; need a min-height check on the input row, or a graceful collapse of one of the other rows.
- If overlay/popup: the popup probably draws on top of the input field; should auto-dismiss when input mode is entered.

## Acceptance

- Reproduce the obscuring at a known terminal size.
- Fix the responsible widget interaction.
- Add a small comment near the fix explaining what was overlapping, so future renderer changes don't re-introduce it.

## Cross-reference

- mu-wd2 (arrow keys) — same area of code; consider tackling together since both touch input-mode rendering.

title: Profile-driven file-edit isolation (generalize worktree isolation pattern)
description:
Generalizes claude-code 2.1.139's background-task worktree-isolation pattern into mu's session-profile model. Memory `fb5cd5be`.

## Motivation

claude-code 2.1.139 isolates background-task file edits to a private worktree, automatically. mu's equivalent should NOT be hard-wired to one task shape (background-only); it should be a property of the session's profile / authority tier. A "Restricted" or "Delegate" profile MAY require an isolated working surface; a "Root" profile does not.

## Scope

In:
- SessionProfile struct gains `file_isolation: FileIsolation { None | Worktree { root: PathBuf } | ReadOnly }`.
- Tool-side enforcement: Edit / Write tools consult the active session's `file_isolation` before resolving paths.
- Default: profiles inherit `None`; the Delegate / Restricted tiers (from mu-ikz, mu-pex) override to `Worktree { ... }`.

Out:
- The worktree-creation lifecycle itself (assume operator-side `jj new`-equivalent).
- Cross-session merge semantics (a follow-up concern once we observe usage).

## Acceptance

* `FileIsolation` type lives in mu-core; serialized in Capability/Profile.
* Edit/Write tools honor isolation; tests cover the three variants.
* Default-tier sessions are unaffected (None ⇒ existing behavior).

## References

- Refines: mu-627
- Memory: `fb5cd5be`
- Companion: `mu-ikz` (authority-coloring; the tier model that drives profile defaults).

title: Wire cache_control markers on system prompt + tool definitions in mu-ai anthropic provider
description:
## Why

mu-pex F5 dashboard hand-test (2026-05-11) confirmed cache% = 0% on a real anthropic-api+haiku run:

- 51,388 input tokens accumulated across 14 tool calls + 1 final response
- 0 cache_read_input_tokens
- 0 cache_creation_input_tokens

The Usage struct (mu-core/src/agent/types.rs) and the dashboard already *report* cache metrics — but mu isn't *marking* prompt content for caching, so the API never caches.

At haiku pricing ($0.80/M input, $0.08/M cached-read), that ask cost ~$0.04 instead of the ~$0.005 it would have cost if the system prompt + tool defs were cached. ~85% savings on a representative mu workload, plus a 30-50% latency reduction on the prompt-processing portion of TTFT.

## What

Add Anthropic-style `cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"}` markers on the long-lived parts of the request body:

1. System prompt (one marker at the end of the system text block)
2. Tool definitions (one marker on the last tool spec, which Anthropic interprets as 'cache everything up to and including this')

These don't need per-call configurability — they're the parts of the request that are stable across asks within a session. The variable parts (per-turn user messages, tool_results) stay uncached.

Anthropic supports up to 4 cache_control breakpoints per request. We use 2; that leaves headroom for future cacheable segments (e.g., a chunked conversation prefix once context windows grow).

## Where

`crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs` (and possibly `anthropic_tests.rs`).

## How to A/B test

This bead is uniquely testable against the work we just landed: run F5 dashboard against this branch and against main, compare the `cache%` column after a session with multiple asks against the same prompt. Expected: 0% on main, 30-90% on this branch depending on how much of the request is the stable prefix vs. per-turn content.

## Acceptance

- Workspace tests still pass.
- Anthropic provider unit tests assert cache_control markers are present on system prompt + tool defs in the request body.
- F5 dashboard cache% column shows nonzero on a multi-ask session against anthropic_api.

## References

- mu-pex F5 dashboard: commit yqoktozo 0d3a3f23
- Anthropic prompt caching docs (external): https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching
- Discovered during 2026-05-11 hand-test (51k input tokens, 0% cache hit)

title: mu-clip-coding-mechanical: 6 mechanical clippy lints in mu-coding (sort_by_key x2, clamp_like_pattern, wildcard_in_or_patterns x2, obfuscated_if_else)
description:
Six clippy lints in mu-coding, all with clear mechanical fixes that clippy itself suggests (`try this:` blocks). None involve design decisions; this is straight refactoring to the suggested forms.

## Items

1. `sort_by_key` (×2 locations) — replace `sort_by(|a, b| a.field.cmp(&b.field))` with `sort_by_key(|x| x.field)`.
2. `clamp_like_pattern` (×1) — replace `x.max(lo).min(hi)` with `x.clamp(lo, hi)`.
3. `wildcard_in_or_patterns` (×2) — replace `Foo | _` with just `_` (wildcard already covers the named variant).
4. `obfuscated_if_else` (×1) — `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/grep.rs:293` — replace `x.then_some("a").unwrap_or("b")` chain with `if x { "a" } else { "b" }`.

Exact locations are emitted by `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features`; suggestion blocks in each error message are drop-in.

## Acceptance

- All 6 mechanical lints resolved per clippy's suggested forms
- cargo clippy passes (for these specific lints) — overall clippy still fails on the other cleanup beads' items
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead

title: Authority-coloring as refinement on top of state-coloring (mu-pex)
description:
Refines mu-pex's per-session state-coloring model with an authority/role layer. Memory `fb5cd5be` (claude-code 2.1.139 → mu mapping) and memory `5ff7511e` (color palette stays coarse — ~3 colors, never more than 5).

## Motivation

mu-pex's state-coloring distinguishes session states (idle / streaming / tool-running / done). claude-code 2.1.139's role/tier concept layers on top: which AUTHORITY does a session carry (root capability, attenuated user capability, delegate capability)? These two axes are independent — a streaming root-authority session looks different from a streaming attenuated-delegate session.

## Scope

In:
- Authority tier enum (Root / User / Delegate / Restricted). 3–4 tiers max — coarse per `5ff7511e`.
- Tier rendered as a coloring overlay (border color, label) ON TOP of the state coloring. Two visual axes: state (fill/animation) + authority (border/label).
- Wiring: SessionStatusSummary gains `authority_tier` derived from `Capability`. TUI list view picks up the tier.

Out:
- Authority tier transitions (delegation already handles this).
- Beyond-coarse palettes (memory `5ff7511e` caps at ~5 colors).

## Acceptance

* `Capability` → `AuthorityTier` projection function with unit tests.
* TUI session list shows authority tier visually distinct from state.
* No more than 4 authority tiers; rationale documented.

## References

- Refines: mu-pex
- Memory: `fb5cd5be` (claude-code 2.1.139 mapping), `5ff7511e` (coarse palette)

title: TUI: expose provider/model selection in 'n' new-session flow
description:
## Why

Sessions in mu's architecture are independent — each carries its own ProviderSelector at create_session time. So having multiple concurrent sessions in one daemon, each with a different provider+model, is already supported wire-side. But the TUI's 'n' (new session) keybind always uses self.default_provider, which is a single tuple set at startup.

Net effect: a feature mu already supports is locked behind a CLI flag (--provider / --model on mu-tui launch) instead of being an in-TUI affordance.

## What

Options, in increasing scope:

A. Palette commands `:provider <kind>` and `:model <id>` that update default_provider for the next 'n' press. Smallest change; trades discoverability for terseness. ~30 LOC.

B. Two-step 'n' flow: 'n' opens a small picker (provider on top, model below, both with keyboard navigation), then create_session fires on Enter. ~80-150 LOC depending on picker polish.

C. Both: palette for keyboard-fast use; picker for discoverability.

Recommend (A) first — it gets the feature usable in ~30 LOC; (B) can layer on later when the rest of the TUI gets palette-/picker-friendlier.

## Acceptance

- Running mu-tui without --provider/--model, then issuing :provider openai_codex / :model gpt-5.5 in the palette, then pressing 'n', creates a session against openai_codex/gpt-5.5.
- Existing default-provider behavior preserved when no palette command is issued.
- Session list (F2 when it lands, or the session row in F1) shows which provider+model each session ran against.

## References

- Default flow today: crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs::App::create_session uses self.default_provider.
- ProviderSelector wire type: crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs::ProviderSelector.

title: Implement mu-035: session.provider_status + cancel_outstanding
description:
Spec at specs/mu-035-session-provider-status.md (jj spouxlsp d69719c3).

Three deliverables:
1. session.provider_status periodic notification with kind (awaiting_first_token | streaming | thinking | tool_executing | awaiting_tool_result | idle), started_at_unix_ms, elapsed_ms, optional bytes_received / tool_call_id.
2. session.cancel_outstanding narrow cancel (kills provider call, session stays alive).
3. daemon.outstanding_calls fleet view.

INV-4 is load-bearing: the emit-tick runs on a tokio timer independent of the provider future, so a stalled provider still produces visible status.

Once landed:
- TUI's phase line gets authoritative ground truth (no more session.list-derived heuristics)
- mu_client.py defensive layer becomes belt-and-suspenders
- Refinement experiment's silent stalls become instantly diagnosable

Estimated 1 week of focused Rust work.

TUI's phase line can now render 'awaiting first token 4.2s' or 'tool: read 8.1s' with growing elapsed_ms. comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Phase C landed in jj lmoxwqvs 7733782d — session.cancel_outstanding RPC. AgentInput::CancelOutstanding + Outcome::OutstandingCancelled sentinel; dispatch handler in handle_cancel_outstanding; abort propagates through stream/tool/approval recv sites; outer loop catches and emits Done(Aborted), keeps session alive. was_in field is placeholder until Phase D plumbs the live tracker.

title: AskSessionRequest: per-ask provider/model override
description:
## Why

Today, a session's (provider, model) is fixed at create_session time. To use a different model for one ask — e.g., "use haiku for this cheap subtask in the middle of a sonnet session" — there's no path short of creating a new session (losing context).

Surfaced 2026-05-11 during a multi-iteration discussion about model routing. The agent-router script (~/src/claude-personal/scripts/agent-router) already does this externally; doing it natively in mu means a session can mix model choices without going through a wrapper.

## What

Add an optional `provider_override` field to AskSessionRequest. When present, the agent loop builds the provider for THIS ask using the override instead of the session's stored provider, but doesn't persist it — the session keeps its original (provider, model). Subsequent asks use the session default again unless overridden.

The 'persistent swap' variant is its own bead (see mu-??? session.set_provider) — this one is per-call only.

## Wire shape (proposal)

```json
{
  "session_id": "session-1",
  "user_message": "...",
  "provider_override": { "kind": "anthropic_api", "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001" }
}
```

## Considerations

- Cost: opens the door to 'route each ask to the cheapest viable model' patterns. This is what the agent-router script does today externally.
- Capability surface: must validate that the override provider supports the tools the session has bound; otherwise error early.
- Cache impact: overriding the model means the previously-cached prefix (system prompt + tools) becomes a cache miss on the override target. The dashboard's cache% column will reflect this naturally.

## Acceptance

- Unit test: AskSessionRequest with provider_override hits the override provider, not the session's stored one.
- The override doesn't mutate session state — subsequent asks without override use the session's original (provider, model).
- F5 dashboard shows the override as its own (provider, model) row (since the daemon.usage_history aggregator groups by provider_kind + model on each call, this should fall out naturally).

title: mu-clip-coding-too-many-args: 3 functions in mu-coding with 9-10 args (clippy::too_many_arguments)
description:
Three functions in mu-coding trigger clippy::too_many_arguments (signatures with 9-10 args; clippy's default threshold is 7). These are signature-design decisions, not mechanical fixes.

## Items (exact locations from clippy)

- 1× function with 10/7 args
- 2× functions with 9/7 args

(Specific locations + names in clippy output; not pre-listed here because the decision per-function may differ — bundle into a config struct, builder pattern, or just `#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]` with rationale.)

## Per-function decision space

For each: choose one of —

A. **Bundle into a config struct.** Most common for ergonomic call sites. Adds one struct + Default. Best when args naturally group (e.g. "render options" struct, "server config" struct).
B. **Builder pattern.** When some args are optional and call sites vary. More boilerplate; usually overkill for 3-arg overflow.
C. **`#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]` with rationale.** When refactoring would harm clarity at call sites (e.g. internal dispatch fns where every arg is mandatory + unrelated). The allow must include a one-line comment naming why.

## Acceptance

- Each of the 3 functions has either: (a) refactored to ≤7 args via struct/builder, OR (b) explicit `#[allow]` with rationale comment
- Decision rationale documented in commit body per-function
- cargo clippy passes (for these specific lints)
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead

## Why this bead is higher-judgment than the mechanical one

Signature changes ripple to every call site. If we go option A or B, we must update all callers. If option C, we admit the function has irreducible complexity. The right answer per function depends on call-site usage patterns — worth a quick survey of each function's callers before deciding.
close_reason: Landed in jj revision omulvzmu. Three config structs (AskOptions, NewSession, BuildSessionRequest<'_>) replace 9-10 arg signatures. 7 call sites updated. Workspace clippy -D warnings + fmt --check + 361 tests all green. CI gate should now pass on the next push.

title: F5 dashboard: add tool_total_ms p95 column
description:
## Why

The mu-pex Phase 1 RPC (`daemon.usage_history`) returns `tool_total_ms` (per-(ToolCall, ToolResult) pair durations, aggregated as median + p95) in every row, but the F5 dashboard's Phase A renderer (mu-xln, commit yqoktozo 0d3a3f23) omits this column. Discovered 2026-05-11 when a 29s/14-tool ask showed 17s of streaming + 116 output tokens — most of the wall was tool execution, and the dashboard couldn't show it.

"Where is the wall time going?" is one of the canonical metrics-dashboard questions. tool_total_ms is the answer for tool-heavy workloads and TTFT / streaming together are the answer for prose-heavy ones. Without the tool column, the dashboard only answers half the question.

## What

In crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs render_usage:

1. Add a 'tool p95' column header between 'wall p95' and 'in tok'.
2. Pull row["tool_total_ms"]["p95"] and format via the existing fmt_ms_p95 helper.
3. Update the widths array to include the new column (8 char width fits 'XXXXms' + spacing).
4. Verify the table still fits in a standard 120-col terminal (current rendered width is ~106 chars + 2 borders; adding 8+1 = ~115, fits).

## Acceptance

- F5 pane shows a 'tool p95' column.
- Empty rows (no tool calls) render '—' or '0ms' via fmt_ms_p95 (it returns '—' on None).
- No layout regression on narrow terminals (test at 80, 100, 120 cols).

## Note on column ordering

Current order favors timing-decomposition reading left to right: ttft p95 → stream p95 → wall p95. Inserting tool p95 between stream and wall preserves that 'what happened during the wall' progression: AwaitingFirstToken time → streaming time → tool time → total wall.
