title: aws-recon tool skeleton: fixture-only aws_recon tool implementing mu-039 pre-dispatch path
description:
First implementation slice of the mu-039 aws-recon skill (spec at `specs/mu-039-aws-recon-skill.md`). Tool-only; no skill-activation wiring yet. No live AWS, no runner subprocess.

## Why this slice (not the whole skill)

mu-039 is large: skill activation, three tool surfaces (`aws_recon`, `aws_plan_from_recon`, `aws_cloudtrail_session_events`), CloudTrail audit-join metadata, runner version capture. Doing it as one bead is a $40+ /goal and crosses too many concerns. This slice exercises the dispatch-gate landed in mu-stw end-to-end on the simplest surface (`aws_recon`) while keeping the boundary that mu-core/mu-coding never reads live AWS credentials.

## Scope

### In

- New tool `aws_recon` registered in `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/`.
- `ToolPolicy::required_aws_capability = Some("aws.scout.readonly".into())` so the mu-stw gate refuses dispatch when the session lacks the grant.
- Input schema (Tool::spec()): `{ capability: String, call_timeout_secs: u32, output_dir: Option<String> }` with defaults consistent with the spec (`specs/mu-039-aws-recon-skill.md:154-162`).
- Pre-dispatch input validation:
  1. session capability contains AwsCapability with matching name (already enforced by mu-stw; this is belt-and-suspenders / better error-shape);
  2. `AwsCapabilityCatalog::resolve_materialized()` succeeds for `input.capability`;
  3. catalog entry has `mutation_allowed == false`.
  Fail-closed: any check failure → `Tool::execute` returns a structured refusal, no runner invocation, audit-shape event emitted.
- **Dry-run/fixture execution mode**: tool reads `MU_AWS_RECON_FIXTURE_DIR` env var. If set, tool returns a stubbed `ReconReport` (artifact path = fixture path); if unset, tool returns `not-yet-implemented` refusal with a structured error. This proves the dispatch path + catalog resolution without standing up the runner.
- Test cases:
  - dispatch refused when session lacks `aws.scout.readonly` (already tested via mu-stw; mirror here at tool granularity);
  - catalog miss → structured error;
  - `mutation_allowed: true` entry → structured error;
  - fixture-mode happy path → ReconReport returned with expected shape;
  - fixture-unset → not-yet-implemented refusal (this branch will be replaced when runner wiring lands).
- Audit metadata captured on every dispatch (success or refusal): mu session id, capability name, catalog digest, local AWS profile name from catalog, role ARN from catalog. No STS metadata yet (fixture mode has none).

### Out

- Skill activation surface (the `SkillActivation`/`ToolSchema` spans that the full mu-039 spec produces). Tool stands alone; operator manually grants `aws.scout.readonly` for tests.
- The other two mu-039 tools (`aws_plan_from_recon`, `aws_cloudtrail_session_events`) — each gets its own bead.
- Actually invoking `scripts/mu-aws-capability-run.sh` + `scripts/aws-recon.sh`. Runner wiring is a follow-on bead so the boundary is clean.
- STS / CloudTrail audit-join. Audit-shape struct lands here but the optional fields stay None in fixture mode.
- biscuit-auth, cross-daemon attenuation.

## Acceptance

- New tool registered; mu-coding exposes it.
- All test cases above pass.
- `cargo test --workspace` green.
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` green.
- `cargo fmt --check` green.
- Commit cites `specs/mu-039-aws-recon-skill.md:<lines>` for the spec references it implements.

## Design notes (decide at impl time)

- Where does the catalog live? mu-core owns the types (mu-ysh). Tool needs an instance. Options: (a) operator pre-loads via env var path; (b) tool takes a catalog handle injected at registration time. Lean (b) so tests can inject in-memory catalogs; env-var path resolution is the operator-facing default that produces a catalog handle at startup.
- ReconReport shape: minimal v1 = `{ artifact_dir: PathBuf, catalog_digest: String, capability: String }`. Real fields land when runner wiring lands.
- Error type: re-use the structured error shape from mu-stw's dispatch refusal so the audit fields are uniform across pre-dispatch refusal and Tool::execute refusal.

## Why this matters

mu-stw landed the dispatch gate but nothing exercises it yet. This bead is the smallest concrete tool that proves: capability gate fires correctly, catalog resolution path works, fixture-mode lets us iterate on tool ergonomics + audit shape without touching live AWS. After this lands, mu-fb0 (live-loop adoption) + the runner-wiring bead can compose independently.

## References

- Spec: `specs/mu-039-aws-recon-skill.md`
- Dispatch gate: `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs` (mu-stw, PR #8)
- Capability catalog: `crates/mu-core/src/aws.rs` (mu-ysh, PR #2)
- Tool surface convention: existing tools in `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/`

title: Pluggable ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy traits
description:
Introduce two orthogonal traits for the rope-based context architecture:

```rust
trait ProviderRenderer { fn render(rope: &RetainedRope, target: ProjectionTarget) -> ProviderMessages; }
trait CacheStrategy { fn boundaries(rope) -> Vec<CacheBoundary>; fn annotate(messages, boundaries); }
```

ProviderRenderer is per-provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, FauxProvider). CacheStrategy is composable: where to put cache boundaries given the rope. Multiple strategies coexist for A/B testing.

## Scope

In: trait defs in crates/mu-core; AnthropicCacheStrategy (boundary at first volatile retained span; cache_control: ephemeral); NoCacheStrategy (no-op for OpenAI/Faux); AnthropicProviderRenderer; tests.

Out: A/B-testing harness; OpenAI impl; adoption in live agent loop (later bead).

## Why

Provider differences (cache, message format, system prompt placement) are scattered. Centralizing behind two traits makes: adding a provider mechanical; cache strategies A/B testable; rope semantics provider-independent.

## Acceptance

Traits compile with at least one impl each; existing Anthropic paths refactored without regression; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-2-provider-cache-strategies.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8
Memories: 3cc7a18c, fb5cd5be (claude-code 2.1.139 validation)
Existing: mu-i6j (cache_control work)
close_reason: Foundational half landed (ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy traits + stub RetainedRope + NoCacheStrategy + FauxProviderRenderer + 17 tests). Provider-specific impls (Anthropic, OpenAI) deferred to follow-on beads.

The TUI surfaces in `mu-pex` (system_prompt indicator) and `mu-jb3` (transcript pane) currently render the agent-side message stream directly. They should source OperatorView output from a `ProviderRenderer::render(rope, OperatorView)` call instead.

## Scope

IN:

- Route them through `FauxProviderRenderer` (or whichever production renderer is in scope by then — mu-bn4 / mu-3aa land later) with `ProjectionTarget::OperatorView`.

title: mu-cap-aws follow-on: intersect_aws_sets HashMap O(n+m) + set-level INV-1 regression test
description:

title: mu-file-watch: implement rope source-change detection via kqueue/inotify (watchman as candidate impl)
description:
The spec amendment at `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` (section 'Span source-change detection via OS file watches', lines 681+) describes the design: spans that reference file content (`FileLoad` SpanKind, landed in mu-1n3) should be invalidated/rehydrated when the underlying file changes. No implementation bead existed; this fills the gap.

Operator preference (2026-05-14): use libevent/kqueue/epoll directly for the primitive. If we later need cross-platform with reliability beyond what direct kqueue gives, **watchman** (Facebook's, used by jj) is the candidate higher-level abstraction.

## Scope

IN:
- Per-FileLoad-span watch registration when the span enters the rope
- OS-native watcher: kqueue on FreeBSD/macOS, inotify on Linux, ReadDirectoryChangesW on Windows
- Watcher emits change events; rope handles via the policy/signal separation already specced (lines 701-707)
- Optional: watchman backend behind a feature flag for environments where the OS-native path has issues

OUT:
- Eager rehydration (re-read every changed file immediately). The signal/policy separation says: watcher emits 'changed' signal; policy decides whether to rehydrate (could be immediate, debounced, or lazy-on-next-projection).
- Watching directories or globs. v1 watches specific files referenced by FileLoad spans.
- Cross-machine file sync (NFS quirks etc.).

## Acceptance

- New FileLoad span registers a watcher on its source path
- File modification fires a 'changed' signal on the rope
- Test: write a file, FileLoad into rope, modify the file, observe the rope emits the change signal
- Policy stub (initially: log + mark stale; full rehydration in a follow-on bead)
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green

## Open design questions

1. **Watcher lifetime**: one global watcher per session, or per-rope, or per-span? Lean: per-session, multiplexing all file watches; cheaper than per-span.
2. **Watchman vs direct kqueue**: start direct; switch to watchman only if scale problems surface (watchman has its own state, runs as a daemon; direct kqueue has zero infrastructure cost).
3. **Cross-platform abstraction**: probably the `notify` crate from crates.io (well-maintained, abstracts kqueue/inotify/Windows). watchman as opt-in fallback.

## References

- Spec: `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` lines 681+ ('Span source-change detection via OS file watches')
- mu-1n3 / mu-ovl: FileLoad SpanKind variant (landed)
- watchman: https://facebook.github.io/watchman/
- notify crate: https://crates.io/crates/notify
- Operator note 2026-05-14: 'I'm fine with libevent/kqueue/epoll for monitoring the files; if we find we have more to use, watchman is a tool that seems to work quite well for jj.'

title: Session persistence across daemon restart
description:
Today sessions live in mu serve's in-memory Sessions map. When mu serve exits (TUI quits → stdin closes → transport exits → sessions drops), every session and its event log are lost.

For a daily-driver TUI that's used across many work sessions, that's a real limitation: you can't 'come back to' the conversation you had yesterday.

What's needed:
  - Event-log persistence (SQLite or JSONL — the event_log.rs design doc references both)
  - SessionDiscovery (mu-038) backend that reads the persisted logs on startup, resurrects SessionInfo entries (the agent loop doesn't need to be re-spawned for a 'cold' session — it spawns on the next ask_session)
  - Cap on retention (per-session age limit? per-session size limit? user choice)
  - Capability state persistence (a session with attenuated capability should keep that on cold reload)

Mentioned as deferred work in mu-036 spec (autonomous loops). Write its own spec when implementation pressure builds — likely after mu-035 Phase B/C/D and mu-037 phase 1 are done.

Workaround for now: keep mu serve running detached; have the TUI connect-to-existing rather than spawn-new. That's not implemented but is a much smaller piece of work than full persistence.

title: mu-tui-markdown: render assistant responses as styled Markdown (termimad or equivalent)
description:
Render assistant Markdown responses in mu's TUI with proper styling: syntax-highlighted code blocks, tables, lists, emphasis, links. Today the TUI renders as plain text. Toggle between rendered and raw views for cut/paste workflow.

## Motivation

Assistant responses ARE Markdown — code blocks, lists, tables, headers, inline code, etc. Rendering them as plain text loses substantial information density. Concrete UX upgrade.

Operator note 2026-05-14: provider/model switching now working end-to-end with streaming updates in both F1 windows. Next-most-impactful TUI win is structured markdown rendering. Operator explicitly requested a raw/rendered toggle so cut/paste can grab the raw source (not the styled-but-unselectable-for-pipe rendering).

## Approach

Rust analogs to Charmbracelet's Glamour (Go):
- `termimad` (https://github.com/Canop/termimad) — most mature Markdown-in-terminal renderer in Rust. Used by broot. Direct dependency candidate.
- `pulldown-cmark` + custom renderer — finer control but more code; only worth it if termimad's defaults don't fit.

Termimad outputs styled text that can be embedded in ratatui via `ratatui::text::Text` / `Line` / `Span`. Compatibility: termimad uses crossterm under the hood, same as ratatui, so colors compose.

## Scope

IN:
- Add `termimad` (or chosen lib) to mu-tui's Cargo.toml
- Render assistant message body through termimad before display
- **Toggle between rendered and raw views** — operator-controlled, per-session. Suggested binding: `R` in transcript view (next to existing F-key family). Default = rendered. Toggle should not lose scroll position or re-flow disruptively.
- Test rendering with: code blocks (multiple languages), tables, nested lists, inline code, emphasis, links
- Theme alignment with mu-u8a's color polish work (moonfly/github_dark family)

OUT:
- Streaming Markdown rendering (rendering partial Markdown as deltas arrive — termimad expects complete blocks). v1: render on session.done event; show raw during streaming. Cleanly avoids the half-rendered-table flicker problem.
- Inline images / VHS-style embedded media

## Design notes

Streaming + raw/rendered interaction: the simplest mental model is "raw while streaming, rendered when done." That gives the operator a stable visual transition (yellow streaming block stays raw text → settles to white styled markdown when complete). The toggle then lets the operator force raw on a completed message for cut/paste. Two states × two phases = matrix to test:

| Phase | Toggle=rendered | Toggle=raw |
|---|---|---|
| Streaming | raw (still streaming) | raw |
| Done | rendered | raw |

This avoids needing partial-markdown rendering altogether and matches what an operator actually wants ergonomically.

Cut/paste compatibility: terminal selection grabs whatever cell content is in the cells. termimad renders styled-text into cells; the *displayed text* is the markdown source minus syntax markers (e.g. `**bold**` → `bold`). For accurate cut/paste of the raw markdown source, toggle to raw first. Document this in the binding's help text.

## Acceptance

- Assistant messages render with at minimum: syntax-highlighted code blocks, formatted tables, styled lists/emphasis
- `R` toggle works in transcript view; default state = rendered
- Toggle preserves scroll position
- Streaming-phase behavior: raw text shown live; transition to rendered (or stay raw if toggle=raw) on session.done
- `cargo test --workspace` green
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Charmbracelet's Glamour (Go): https://github.com/charmbracelet/glamour — the design reference
- termimad (Rust): https://github.com/Canop/termimad
- pulldown-cmark (Rust): https://github.com/raphlinus/pulldown-cmark
- Theme coordination: mu-u8a (TUI color polish — moonfly/github_dark family)
- Pattern shelf: mu-zh1 (TUI references including Charmbracelet design language)

## Notes

title: Thread system_prompt from CreateSessionRequest to Provider::stream
description:
## Why

`CreateSessionRequest.system_prompt: Option<String>` exists at the wire protocol layer (crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs:66) but is **accepted at the API boundary and silently dropped**. The Provider::stream trait signature only takes (messages, tools); no system prompt parameter. So clients can send the field expecting customization and get no effect.

Surfaced 2026-05-11 while implementing mu-i6j (cache_control markers on anthropic provider request). The implementation comments explicitly note this gap:

> System prompt is the other classic cache target but mu doesn't currently thread `system_prompt` from CreateSessionRequest through to Provider::stream — see protocol.rs:66 for the wire field; the body construction does not include a `system` key. When that gap is closed, add a second cache_control marker on the last system content block.

See commit ykumwozs 77bb0ce4.

## What

1. Plumb `system_prompt: Option<String>` from the session manager into AgentLoop / Provider invocations. Existing call sites: dispatch::handle_create_session, sessions storage, agent loop's call-out to the provider.
2. Extend Provider::stream trait signature with system_prompt parameter, OR store it on the AgentConfig (preferred — keeps the trait stable and lets each provider decide how to render it).
3. In anthropic provider's build_request_body: if system_prompt is Some, include a top-level `system` field with a content-block array containing the prompt + cache_control marker (mirroring how tools are cached in mu-i6j).
4. Other providers (openai_codex, openrouter, faux) get equivalent treatment.

## Acceptance

- A session created with system_prompt: Some("...") sees the prompt reach the LLM (verifiable by asking 'what's your system prompt' and seeing the contents).
- For anthropic_api, the F5 dashboard's cache% column rises further (system prompt joins tools as a cached prefix).
- Workspace tests still pass; new tests assert the wire-level translation.

## Out of scope

- Designing what mu's default system prompt should be. (Today's behavior is "no system prompt"; this bead preserves that as the default by treating None == today's behavior.)

title: Skills and tool schemas as rope spans (eliminate parallel loading mechanisms)
description:
Refactor skill activation and tool registration to emit spans into the rope rather than maintain separate loaded-state structures.

## Scope

In: SkillManager emits skill.activated { skill_id, span_refs } events; ToolRegistry represents each tool's schema as an addressable span; capability attenuation becomes a pointer-set filter; RetainedRope API gains activate_skill / deactivate_skill / filter_tools / provenance.

Out: span granularity policy (start per-file, refine later); skill activation triggers (keep current); operator-view UI changes (handled in mu-NEW-4).

## Why

Today skill loading and tool registration are separate code paths from context management. Unifying them: activation/deactivation IS pointer-set membership change. Subagent capability filtering reuses existing rope machinery; provenance built in.

## Acceptance

Skill-activation behavior preserved; tool schemas render identically at agent-view; capability attenuation produces observable pointer-set diff; provenance lookup works; tests for activate/deactivate, filtered tool sets, span provenance; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-3-skills-tools-as-spans.md

title: TUI: suppress empty 'assistant' block on tool-only turns
description:
Currently when the model emits a turn that is entirely tool calls (no text content blocks), the transcript renders a 'assistant — (no text in this turn)' block above the magenta tool block. That's cosmetic cruft.

Fix in crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs::render_transcript_lines: when an 'assistant_message_event' has empty joined text AND there's a 'tool_call' or 'tool_result' event in the same logical turn (i.e. next significant event in the log is a tool_call before the next done), skip rendering the empty assistant block entirely. The tool block itself is the visible record of what the model did this turn.

title: Agent-view vs operator-view projections from the rope
description:
Introduce ProjectionTarget enum (AgentView | OperatorView) and two distinct rendering paths from the same retained pointer set. ProviderRenderer takes a target.

## Scope

In: ProjectionTarget enum; OperatorViewRenderer trait; per-span-kind operator-view rules (tool result -> structured table; skill activation -> one-line badge; tool schema -> name; memory injection -> collapsible); TUI changes (mu-pex / mu-jb3) consume operator-view rendering; tests for both targets.

Out: drill-down UI for inspecting agent-view at specific turn; operator-view variations (compact/detailed/debug) — start with one canonical view.

## Why

LLM tools conflate agent view and operator view. Humans and models have different attention budgets and relevance criteria. Separating projections: operator gets usable surface (skimmable tool results, collapsible context); agent gets full fidelity; debugging becomes possible (AgentView at turn N).

## Acceptance

AgentView produces output identical to current agent context rendering; OperatorView produces distinct usable rendering; TUI integrates (at least mu-pex system_prompt indicator sources operator-view); provenance preserved for span-click drill-down; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-4-projection-targets.md

title: Add mu release binary to stable PATH (or symlink) for agent-router codex backend
description:
## Why

Overnight session tried to delegate mu-036 spec analysis to GPT via `agent-router --auth codex-oauth` (per the routing policy that says 'lean on GPT for spec-heavy tasks'). The router's codex backend expects `mu` to be on PATH for its mu_client integration. mu's release binary lives at `~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu` but isn't symlinked or PATH-exposed.

Prepending PATH inline (`PATH="$HOME/src/public_github/mu/target/release:$PATH" agent-router ...`) works one-shot but doesn't survive the next shell or programmatic invocation from a script that doesn't think to set PATH.

## What

Two reasonable options:
1. Symlink the release binary: `ln -sf ~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu ~/.local/bin/mu`. Lives alongside claude-code at `~/.local/bin` (per memory `reference_claude_binary.md` pattern). Risk: rebuilding the workspace overwrites the binary in-place, so the symlink stays valid — but a `cargo clean` breaks it. Acceptable; rerunning `cargo build --release` restores it.
2. Add the path to zsh PATH via `~/.zsh/zenvironment` (the canonical PATH-setup source per the secrets-config layout we already use). Always works; doesn't need a re-symlink after `cargo clean`.

I'd go with (2) — it's the same pattern `tq` and other build-time-installed binaries use already.

## Acceptance

`mu serve --help` works from a fresh terminal without PATH manipulation. `agent-router --auth codex-oauth ...` works without the user prepending PATH. The session-router falls back gracefully (already does — errors say 'No such file or directory: mu') if the binary is genuinely missing.

title: Metrics framework: per-call time decomposition + quality proxies
description:
Design + implementation arc for mu's ongoing observability. Came out of refinement-experiment discussion 2026-05-11: per-request hard metrics vs per-request quality metrics are different beasts, and we should be deliberate about which we track and why.

Captured in commit/results doc: refinement arm 0/3 forbidden vs baseline 3/3, at 2.2x wall time. That cost is fine for delegation-style work but expensive for interactive — we want to be able to SEE the cost decomposition (TTFT, streaming, tool time, thinking) rather than just total wall.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Two classes of metrics, very different to track
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Hard metrics (always trackable, no ground truth):
  - TTFT = AwaitingFirstToken → Streaming transition (mu-035)
  - Streaming duration
  - ToolExecuting duration (sum)
  - Wall end-to-end
  - Tokens in/out, cache hit %, tool count
  - Per-provider error rate

These don't tell us correctness but they DO catch drift: median TTFT per provider per hour would catch 'codex is stalled' in minutes instead of the 14h silence we had on 2026-05-10/11.

Quality metrics (need fixture):
  - Success rate vs ground truth
  - Refinement passes to clean
  - Cost-to-correct = total tokens to 1 successful response

These need a benchmark fixture (like the spec-summary task in protocol-eval). Outside fixtures, every request is unique.

Proxy metrics (work without ground truth):
  - Refinement-fire rate (if we run grader on everything)
  - User-marked retry rate (TUI accept/reject affordance)
  - Tool error rate (model-induced vs system-induced)

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Implementation arc
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Now-easy (post mu-035 A/B/C + mu-038):
  - daemon.usage_history projection RPC returning median/p95 of
    TTFT / streaming_duration / tool_duration / wall, grouped by
    (provider, model, time-bucket).
  - Backed by aggregating ProviderStatus events from the event log
    (or live tracker snapshots once tracker is in Sessions — Phase D).

Soonish (mu-xln existing P2 bead — F5 Usage Dashboard):
  - Renders the daemon.usage_history projection.
  - Per-provider/model rows with TTFT p95, streaming duration p95,
    tool-call-count distribution.
  - Cost estimate column once we have a per-provider price table.

Later (quality proxies):
  - TUI affordance: '👍 helpful' / '👎 not what I wanted' / 'try again'
    after each session.done.
  - Capture in event log (new EventPayload variant: UserFeedback).
  - Surface as a quality-proxy column in F5.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Cross-references
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Depends on:
  - mu-iwq Phase D (daemon.outstanding_calls + ProviderStatusTracker
    in Sessions) — for snapshot enumeration. Phase A/B/C already
    give us the per-call event stream.
  - mu-038 projection-query patterns — same code area, same style.

Composes with:
  - mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — its data source.
  - mu-036 autonomous loop — refinement-fire-rate is a great
    quality proxy for autonomous sessions.

Not in scope here:
  - Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens). Should be its
    own small bead because it's an updated-rarely lookup table.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

When to implement: not blocking daily-driver. Worth picking up
after mu-iwq Phase D lands or as a parallel slice if a slow
provider catches our attention again.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Per-model corrections rate is a great proxy metric to add to the design — break down refinement_fire_rate by (provider, model) and you get a comparative quality fingerprint. Different models have different priors (today's experiment confirmed gpt-5.5 has a strong login/logout prior; haiku-4.5 may have different ones). Tracking corrections_per_call by model surfaces:
  - which models are best for which task types (factual extraction, code review, etc.)
  - whether a model is degrading over time (vendor pushed an update? prior drift?)
  - cost-of-correctness tradeoff (cheaper-but-more-corrections vs more-expensive-but-fewer)

Add to the F5 dashboard sketch: per-(provider, model) row showing corrections_per_100_calls when grader was enabled. Surfaces 'haiku gets 5/100 wrong on summarisation; gpt-5.5 gets 33/100; sonnet gets 2/100' or whatever the truth turns out to be.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1 (A-scope) landed in commit rwyyuout 28c677c6 (`feat(mu-pex): daemon.usage_history projection RPC — Phase 1 (A-scope)`).

What landed:
- New RPC `daemon.usage_history` with (since_unix_ms?, until_unix_ms?, time_bucket_ms?) request params, returns rows grouped by (provider, model, time-bucket).
- Per-row distributions (median+p95): tool_total_ms, wall_ms, model_call_latency_ms (proxy: AssistantMessageEvent.ts − ContextAssembly.ts).
- Per-row sums: input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, reasoning_tokens, tool_call_count.
- Per-row counts: session_count, error_count.
- Aggregates over in-memory sessions visible via `sessions.snapshot_for_listing()`.
- 12 new unit tests; full workspace test suite green (289 passed, 1 ignored).

Explicitly None in Phase 1 (will land in Phase 1.5 / Iteration 2):
- `ttft_ms` — needs durable `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` (currently wire-only mu-035).
- `streaming_ms` — same reason.

Phase 1.5 plan (queued in task list):
1. Add `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` variant (mu-core/src/event_log.rs).
2. Extend `forwarder.rs::to_log_event` to map `AgentEvent::ProviderStatus` → that variant alongside the existing wire-side mapping.
3. Update `payload_kind_str` in dispatch.rs and the TUI's sidechannel branch in render_transcript_lines.
4. Extend `usage_history::extract_per_session_metrics` to compute TTFT (AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming transition) and streaming_ms (time spent in Streaming per call).
5. Populate the two Option<PercentileStats> fields in UsageHistoryRow.

Still out of scope at the bead level:
- mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — UI consumer of this RPC.
- UserFeedback EventPayload + TUI affordance.
- Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens) — its own follow-up bead.
- Cross-daemon and disk-only historical coverage — gated on mu-iwq Phase D + mu-935 FileBackend.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1.5 landed in commit lrslqwuv b0944d40 — durable ProviderStatusUpdate + TTFT/streaming populated.

Changes:
- New `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` variant (mu-core/src/event_log.rs) mirroring the wire-side `ProviderStatusEvent` minus session_id.
- forwarder `to_log_event` now maps `AgentEvent::ProviderStatus` → that variant. Wire-side `translate_event` unchanged: both projections still derive from the same AgentEvent source — they no longer disagree about durability.
- payload_kind_str (dispatch) + derive_status_from_events (discovery) + render_transcript_lines sidechannel (mu-tui) all extended to handle the new variant.
- `usage_history::extract_per_session_metrics` now walks ProviderStatusUpdate events; consecutive transition emissions (elapsed_ms == 0) yield duration samples for the prior state. Periodic ticks (elapsed_ms > 0) are skipped — they share started_at with their opening transition.
- `aggregate_into_rows` populates `row.ttft_ms` and `row.streaming_ms` via `percentile_stats` over pooled samples. Both stay None when a group has no samples — same shape as Phase 1.

Tests added (+6 over Phase 1, total now 295 active / 1 ignored):
- extraction_computes_ttft_from_awaiting_first_token_transition
- extraction_periodic_ticks_do_not_close_a_state_period
- extraction_records_streaming_periods_separately (interrupted Streaming yields 2 samples)
- extraction_unterminated_state_period_is_dropped (in-flight or aborted)
- aggregation_populates_ttft_and_streaming_in_row
- forwarder::log_records_provider_status_update

Still queued (parking-lot, not in any commit):
- mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — UI consumer of daemon.usage_history.
- TUI feedback affordance (👍/👎/try again) + `UserFeedback` EventPayload variant — quality proxy.
- Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens) — its own follow-up bead (out-of-scope per the original bead).
- Cross-daemon / disk-only historical coverage — gated on mu-iwq Phase D + mu-935 FileBackend.
- Per-comment from 2026-05-11 20:14: per-(provider, model) corrections_per_100_calls when grader enabled — needs refinement integration first.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1.5 follow-up fix in commit sunvrnkp 2b84e8b1 — discovered via hand-test against anthropic-api+haiku.

Two bugs in the initial Phase 1.5 transition-detection logic (both in extract_per_session_metrics):

1. **The transition filter `elapsed_ms == 0` was too strict.** The agent loop emits the AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming transition with elapsed_ms set to call_started.elapsed() (loop_.rs:880), not 0. The filter silently dropped that emission. Net effect: `ttft_ms.count` was counting gaps *between asks* (the only AFT→AFT pair the filter saw); `streaming_ms.count` was always 0.

2. **No Done-driven close-out.** The agent loop doesn't emit a Streaming→Idle transition on session.done. One ask's open Streaming state would bleed into the inter-ask gap before the next AFT entry, polluting streaming_ms with wait time.

Fix: identify transitions by started_at_unix_ms uniqueness (ticks within a period share their period's started_at; transitions always introduce a new one); close out pending state on Done using the Done's timestamp.

Hand-test now produces credible numbers:
- ttft_ms p95 = 1184ms for haiku via anthropic-api (TTFT-dominated, as expected for tiny prompts)
- streaming_ms p95 = 62ms for 5-token responses (~10ms/token)
- wall_ms matches Done.elapsed_ms exactly

Tests added: extraction_streaming_entry_with_nonzero_elapsed_still_counts + extraction_done_closes_out_pending_state_between_asks. Workspace tests: 297 active, 1 ignored.

title: Architecture: amend event-sourced-context.md with skill/tool/cache/projection/subagent/file-watch extensions
providers hardcode max_tokens=4096 regardless of model — truncates Opus/Sonnet responses
description:
Both Anthropic and OpenRouter providers in mu hardcode `\"max_tokens\": 4096` on every request, regardless of model. Opus 4.7 supports up to 32768 output tokens; Haiku 4.5 supports up to 8192. Truncating every response at 4096 wastes the available budget and causes visible cut-offs.

## Code locations

- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:204` — `\"max_tokens\": 4096`
- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openrouter.rs:200` — `\"max_tokens\": 4096`
- Tests pin to 4096:
  - `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic_tests.rs:45`
  - `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openrouter_tests.rs:119`

## Symptom

Opus 4.7 session 2026-05-14, event log `03b75bc4a9c48f82`: workspace-review response cut off mid-sentence after ~11k characters of structured analysis. Final turn's API stop_reason was `\"max_tokens\"` (confirming the cap was hit, not natural completion).

Haiku session earlier same day exhibited the same truncation shape on a similar prompt.

## Fix options (lean toward B)

### A. Bump the constant

Change 4096 → 16384 globally. Cheap, one-line. Downside: Haiku gets a larger budget than its native max (which would be clamped to 8192 server-side anyway, but it's sloppy).

### B. Model-aware max_tokens lookup (recommended)

Add a small helper `max_tokens_for_model(model: &str) -> u32`:
- haiku-4-5* → 8192
- sonnet-4-6* → 8192 (verify)
- opus-4-7* → 16384 (or 32768 for max use of Opus's capacity)
- unknown → 4096 (current default; safe fallback)

Used by both Anthropic and OpenRouter providers. Lives near the model-name normalization code if it exists, else its own module.

### C. Per-session config override

Operator-set max_tokens per session via `:max_tokens N` colon-command or RPC. Composes with B as a per-session override of the lookup default. Defer to follow-on bead unless trivial.

## Scope

IN:
- Implement option B: model-aware max_tokens lookup.
- Both providers use the helper.
- Tests updated; new test asserts opus model gets > 4096; new test asserts unknown model still gets safe fallback.

OUT:
- Per-session override (option C) — separate bead if wanted.
- Auto-continuation on truncation — separate decision.
- TUI \"response truncated\" badge — depends on mu-s5h fix first.

## Acceptance

- `max_tokens_for_model` helper exists, tested.
- Anthropic + OpenRouter providers use it.
- Existing tests updated to reflect the new defaults for their fixture models.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Anthropic API max_tokens docs: model-specific maxima (Opus 4.7 = 32768 in current API; Haiku 4.5 = 8192)
- Event log showing real truncation: `/home/tcovert/.local/share/mu/events/03b75bc4a9c48f82/session-1.jsonl`
- Composes with mu-s5h: the stop_reason aggregation bug; once both land, operator can both see WHY the response stopped AND have enough budget for it not to stop prematurely

title: bare-equivalent boot mode for mu (minimal-context boot for delegates and sub-sessions)
description:
Implements a minimal-context boot mode for mu, equivalent to claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` flag. Memory `fb5cd5be`.

## Motivation

Today every mu session boots with the full operator default: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory context injection, plugin sync, hooks. Most of this is wrong for delegate / sub-session use — a delegate that exists to answer one well-scoped question doesn't need the operator's accumulated memory or the hook configuration.

claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` mode is the same idea: a different *initial retention set* (per the rope amendment in mu-qk8). The rope architecture says this should be declarative — a profile property, not a flag.

## Scope

In:
- SessionBootMode { Full | Bare } (or as a BootProfile carrying which spans to load).
- Bare mode skips: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory injection, plugin/hook sync.
- Bare mode keeps: tool registry, capability, transport.
- Plumbing: create_session and delegate_session accept the boot mode; default is Full for explicit user-driven creates, Bare for delegates by default (with override).
- Tests: a bare session has no CLAUDE.md spans and no auto-memory in its initial retained set.

Out:
- Per-source granularity (skip CLAUDE.md but keep hooks, etc.) — start with one coarse switch; refine if usage shows it's needed.
- Operator-view UI for boot-mode selection — assume CLI / RPC param for now.

## Acceptance

* Boot mode selectable via API (create_session + delegate_session).
* Bare-mode session's initial retained span set is observably smaller (test).
* Default for delegate_session is Bare; create_session default stays Full.
* No regression in existing Full-mode behavior.

## References

- Memory: fb5cd5be (claude-code 2.1.139 mapping)
- Architecture: rope amendment in specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md, Cache-boundary alignment section (the --bare interpretation as a different initial retention set).
- Companions: mu-ikz (authority tier), mu-hlf (file isolation) — together these three constitute the 2.1.139 profile / authority feature mapping.

title: agent loop hardcodes Done.stop_reason — masks MaxTokens/StopSequence from per-turn stream
description:
The agent loop in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs` always emits `AgentEvent::Done { stop_reason: StopReason::EndTurn, ... }` (or `StopReason::Aborted` for cancellations). It never propagates the provider's per-turn `StopReason` (e.g. `MaxTokens`, `StopSequence`) into the session-level Done event.

## Symptom observed

Opus 4.7 session against mu, 2026-05-14, event log `03b75bc4a9c48f82`:
- Last `assistant_message_event` payload: `stop_reason: "max_tokens"` ← real
- `done` event payload: `stop_reason: "end_turn"` ← misleading projection

The Opus response was cut off mid-sentence (max_tokens cap of 4096 hit). The TUI's "session done" affordance, which reads the Done event, reported it as a clean end_turn — making the truncation invisible to the operator. Same pattern would have appeared with Haiku at the same cap.

## Code locations

All `AgentEvent::Done` emissions in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs`:
- `:645` — iteration cap hit → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:738` — OutstandingCancelled → `StopReason::Aborted` (correct)
- `:805` — another cancel path → `StopReason::Aborted` (correct)
- `:992` — normal completion → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:1043` — another completion path → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:1068` — Outcome::Done helper → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`

The provider returns `AssistantMessage` with a real `stop_reason` (Anthropic provider maps `\"max_tokens\"` → `StopReason::MaxTokens` correctly at `anthropic.rs:344`), but the agent loop discards it.

## Why this matters

Event-sourced context architecture treats projections as derived from the event stream. The `done` event is supposed to reduce per-turn signal into a session-level summary. When the reduction loses information that the source preserved, the projection is wrong. This is exactly the failure mode the architecture is designed to prevent — finding it here means a regression test discipline gap.

Operator impact: a truncated response that looks complete in the TUI. Bigger consequence: any tool/automation downstream of the Done event makes wrong decisions about whether to retry or extend.

## Scope

IN:
- Propagate the final turn's `StopReason` from `assistant_msg.stop_reason` into all `Done` emissions where a turn just completed.
- Hold the latest-known stop_reason in the agent loop's local state, updated each turn.
- For paths where the loop terminates without a final turn (iteration cap, no provider call), keep current behavior (`EndTurn` / `Aborted`) but document why.
- Test: per-turn stop_reason of MaxTokens / StopSequence / EndTurn propagates correctly into the Done event.
- Test: cancellation path still emits Aborted regardless of provider stop_reason.

OUT:
- TUI rendering improvements for the new state (separate bead — operator badge for "response was truncated").
- Auto-continuation policy (\"if max_tokens hit, ask the model to continue\" — separate decision).

## Acceptance

- Done event's stop_reason matches the underlying per-turn provider stop_reason on the natural-completion path.
- New regression tests in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_tests.rs`.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Provider stop_reason mapping: `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:344`
- StopReason enum: `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs`
- Symptom event log: `/home/tcovert/.local/share/mu/events/03b75bc4a9c48f82/session-1.jsonl`

title: mu-edit-patch: diff/patch as a first-class editing primitive (complement to line-based Edit tool)
description:
Today, mu's Edit tool is line-based string replacement (old_string → new_string). This works but: (1) multi-hunk edits require many separate tool calls, (2) ambiguous matches force the model to include lots of surrounding context, (3) some patterns are easier to express as unified diffs than as old/new pairs.

Aider's approach: the model emits unified diffs; the harness applies them via patch-like logic. More robust for multi-hunk + structural edits. Worth a complementary primitive (NOT a replacement for Edit; both have their uses).

## Scope

IN:
- New `Patch` (or `ApplyDiff`) tool in `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/`
- Input: unified diff string OR (file_path, hunks: [{old_lines, new_lines, position}]) — pick one
- Application logic: use the `similar` or `diffy` crate from crates.io; fail cleanly when hunks don't apply (return position + context)
- Returns: success/failure per hunk, list of applied hunks, list of rejected hunks with reason
- Same Read-required convention as Edit

OUT:
- Replacing Edit. Both stay; agents pick the right one for the job.
- Conflict resolution (3-way merge). v1 is straight apply-or-reject.
- Cross-file refactors (single-file patches only).

## Acceptance

- New `Patch`/`ApplyDiff` tool registered in mu-coding
- Schema documents the input format choice
- At least three test cases: clean apply, ambiguous apply, rejected hunk with structured error
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green

## Design fork (decide at impl time)

Two reasonable input shapes:

A. **Unified diff string** — matches `patch(1)`'s format. Familiar to humans. Model emits text that LLMs are good at generating. Implementation parses the diff format.

B. **Structured hunks JSON** — schema-validated input. Less natural for the model to emit but easier to validate. Implementation skips parsing.

Lean A: aider's experience shows unified diffs work well in practice. The downside (parsing fragility) is mitigated by the `similar`/`diffy` crates.

## References

- Aider's diff approach: https://aider.chat/docs/more-info.html (their edit format docs)
- similar crate: https://crates.io/crates/similar
- diffy crate: https://crates.io/crates/diffy
- mu's existing Edit tool: `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/edit.rs`
- Architecture gap noted 2026-05-14 alongside mu-web-fetch + mu-file-watch

title: mu-tui: predicted-next-user-message autocomplete (CC-style grayed prefill)
description:
Add a Claude-Code-style "predicted next user message" affordance to mu's TUI input buffer. After each assistant turn completes, render a grayed-text suggestion of the user's likely next prompt. Accept with right-arrow + Enter (or Tab); ignore by typing over it.

## Why

Operator (tcovert) reports CC's autocomplete-prediction feature has materially changed his input patterns — he now frequently accepts the suggestion with → + Enter rather than typing. Since mu is positioned as a daily-driver alternative to CC, parity here is a meaningful UX win.

Operator notes: "if you've noticed more terse responses from me that is why."

## Background: feature is client-side, not API-shipped

Verified 2026-05-14 via claude-code-guide agent: Anthropic's Messages API response schema (`content`, `id`, `model`, `role`, `stop_reason`, `usage`) does NOT include any "predicted next user message" / "suggested reply" field. CC's feature is entirely client-side.

CC's own docs (https://code.claude.com/docs/en/how-claude-code-works) hint at a heuristic approach: "surface likely next prompts based on your repo's git history and recent work." This may describe the startup prompt-suggestion feature, not the post-turn autocomplete — ambiguity remains in the published docs.

For mu we have to build it ourselves. We cannot extract it from a provider response.

## Design fork (decide at impl time)

### Option A: Cheap-model inference call

After each assistant turn completes, fire a separate Messages-API call with the conversation history + a "predict the most likely next user message" prompt to a cheap model (Haiku 4.5, or whatever the cheapest configured provider exposes). Return result to TUI as soon as it lands.

Pros:
- Higher-quality, context-aware suggestions
- Composes with multi-provider router (mu-aware: use whichever model is cheapest per session)
- Eventually composes with the operator's planned local-LLM-on-Blackwell setup — predictions become free + fast

Cons:
- Token cost per turn (small but real; Max5 OAuth makes it free until weekly cap)
- Latency: Haiku first-token typically ~500-800ms; might land late
- Failure mode: provider hiccup → no suggestion

### Option B: Local heuristic (no LLM call)

Static analysis over recent conversation state + git status + transcript patterns. Examples: "ok", "go ahead", "no, do X instead", common short replies after a question. Library of templated continuations matched to assistant-turn shape (did it ask a question? did it propose a plan?).

Pros:
- Zero token cost
- Instant (<10ms)
- No provider dependency

Cons:
- Lower-quality suggestions; won't capture nuance
- Won't predict longer multi-sentence operator inputs that the LLM approach might

### Lean: A, with B as fallback when no provider configured

The operator's daily-driver setup will always have a provider configured. A gives the higher-quality experience and composes well with the planned local-LLM-on-Blackwell future (predictions become zero-cost when a local model serves the prediction role). B stays as a useful no-network fallback.

## Scope

### In

- New mu-core RPC: `predict_next_user_message(session_id) -> Option<String>` triggered on session.done events.
- Implementation routes through the existing provider abstraction (same code path as the main assistant call, different cheap model + system prompt).
- TUI: render returned suggestion in dim color in the input buffer; accept with → or Tab; clear on any other keypress.
- Configurable per-session: enable/disable, model override, max latency before giving up.
- Settles cleanly: if the user starts typing before suggestion arrives, suggestion is dropped, no flicker.

### Out

- Multi-suggestion (CC has only one; mu can match that).
- Predictive caching of full responses (just the next user prompt, not the assistant's reply to it).
- Learning from accept/reject history (could be a follow-on bead).
- Operator's planned local-LLM-on-Blackwell hosting (separate infra bead).

## Open questions

1. **What's CC's actual latency target?** Worth observing empirically — measure how fast the grayed text appears after an assistant turn ends in CC. If it's <300ms consistently, that points to local heuristic; if 500-1500ms, that points to cheap-LLM call.
2. **Does mu's existing provider routing have a "cheapest available" selector?** If not, this needs a small extension.
3. **Should the prediction prompt include recent project context (git status, open beads)?** CC's docs suggest yes; for mu we have richer state (mu's own event log) we could feed in.

## Acceptance

- mu-core RPC implemented + tested (mocked provider call in unit tests).
- mu-tui renders suggestion in dim color; accept-with-→-or-Tab works; typing over discards cleanly.
- Per-session config respected.
- Operator can A/B the suggestion-on vs suggestion-off TUI experience for a real session.
- `cargo test --workspace` green; `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt --check` green.

## References

- CC docs page describing the autocomplete affordance: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/how-claude-code-works
- Operator's observation: this conversation, 2026-05-14 session
- Future composition: local LLM on Blackwell once that hardware lands

title: Update rustls-webpki to fix 3 dependabot alerts (1 high, 2 low)
description:
GitHub dependabot flagged 3 alerts on main (all rustls-webpki, transitive via reqwest → rustls):

  high  GHSA-82j2-j2ch-gfr8  DoS panic on malformed CRL BIT STRING
  low   GHSA-xgp8-3hg3-c2mh  Wildcard name constraints accepted
  low   GHSA-965h-392x-2mh5  URI name constraints incorrectly accepted

Real risk for mu: modest. We talk TLS outbound to anthropic.com / chatgpt.com / openrouter.ai (well-known services). We don't process CRLs ourselves. High-severity DoS would need a malicious server in the chain.

Fix: cargo update -p rustls-webpki. Test the workspace still builds + tests pass. P3 because workaround = trusted upstreams.

title: Per-session JSONL event log on disk (write-side persistence)
description:
mu serve writes every SessionEvent to a per-session JSONL file as it's appended to the in-memory event log. No read-back-at-restart logic — that's mu-mh4 Phase 2. Write-only.

Motivation: when sharing TUI test sessions with Claude (or any other agent / human reviewer), an on-disk log is faster, more accurate (no copy-paste truncation), and structurally typed (JSONL parses, terminal screenshots don't).

Scope:
  - Path: ~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon_id>/<session_id>.jsonl
  - One serde-encoded SessionEvent per line
  - Append-only; no rotation in v1 (mu sessions are bounded by daemon lifetime)
  - Daemon-id directory means concurrent daemons coexist cleanly
  - Write fails do NOT block in-memory append (log fsync delay must not stall the agent loop)

Implementation:
  - SessionEventLog gains an optional disk_writer field
  - serve_with_io creates the file on session insert
  - On append(), write the encoded line; ignore IO errors (best-effort)
  - File opens for append on first event, drops on session removal

Composes with mu-mh4: this is the FOUNDATION for cold-reload. Once write-side is durable, mu-mh4 Phase 2 adds the read-at-startup loop + SessionDiscovery resurrection.

Composes with mu-pex (metrics framework): the per-session JSONL files become a natural source for offline analysis (median TTFT per provider, cost-of-correctness over time, etc.) without needing a live daemon connection.

Also useful for protocol-eval — instead of stitching events back together from notifications, the analysis layer can just walk the JSONL files.

Filed P2; small implementation (30-60 min).

title: Periodic codex smoke-test: validate mu-035 heartbeat catches backend stalls
description:
## Why

mu-035 Phase B added periodic ProviderStatusUpdate ticks while in non-streaming waits specifically so a stalled provider stays visible instead of hanging silently. The classic motivating incident was the 14hr openai-codex stall observed 2026-05-10/11 (see mu-cea). Validating that the heartbeat actually catches a recurrence — and how fast — needs running codex regularly enough to hit a stall in practice.

Filed as a behavioral / monitoring task: not new code, but a habit + a place to record observations.

## What

Use openai-codex as the periodic provider for at least 1 ask/day during normal mu testing. Watch the F5 dashboard:
- ttft p95 for openai_codex should look like other providers' (sub-second to a few seconds typical).
- If a stall is in progress, the periodic ProviderStatusUpdate ticks should keep arriving (visible in F8 firehose) even when the model isn't sending content.
- Wall p95 should be the slowest signal; if it diverges wildly from ttft+stream+tool, that's a stall signature.

If a stall recurs:
1. Reopen mu-cea (or file a new bead) with the date + duration + recovery mechanism.
2. Check whether mu-035's heartbeat fired during the stall (it should — if not, that's a separate bug).
3. Verify session.cancel_outstanding (mu-035 Phase C) works to abort cleanly.

## Cross-reference

- mu-cea: BUG / external — openai-codex backend stalls
- mu-035: provider_status heartbeat
- The heartbeat is the load-bearing safety net here; the only way to know it works is to actually use codex regularly.

title: Future: $EDITOR prompt-edit audit events
description:
When mu supports $EDITOR for full prompt editing, prefer supervisor/UI-owned editor launch rather than launching arbitrary editors inside constrained workers. The worker asks the supervisor/broker for a prompt edit; supervisor creates a controlled temp file, launches $EDITOR, reads the result, then submits finalized text.

Consider durable events:
- PromptEditRequested
- PromptEditStarted { path/editor }
- PromptEditCompleted { sha256_before, sha256_after, bytes, duration_ms }
- PromptSubmitted

This preserves provenance while avoiding giving Capsicum-constrained workers broad editor/plugin/shell authority.

title: goal-per-step-verification: /goal workers verify tool-call outcomes before continuing
description:
Standing discipline addition to the goal-protocol skill: autonomous /goal workers must verify every tool-call outcome before consuming the result downstream. The verification step is what prevents silent-success-of-actually-failed operations from compounding into wrong-path budget burns.

## Load-bearing argument (compounding-failure math)

Per harness-engineering.ai (and intuited from our own experiments): if each tool call succeeds 95% of the time and failures don't self-correct, a 20-step task succeeds 0.95^20 = 36% of the time. Verification at each step (e.g., schema check + precondition revalidation) restores end-to-end completion to ~96%, per their cited data.

Our own /goal experiments confirm the failure mode:

- **Experiment 4 (failed mu-ovl-A)** burned  cap on 11+ identical Edit retries because the Edit error 'File has not been read yet' wasn't verified before retry. Loop-guard halted the spin at the wrapper level, not the worker level.
- **Experiment 5 attempt 2 (permission-mode deadlock)** spun on Bash permission prompts. Same shape: tool failure not verified → retry with no change → loop-guard caught it.

Per-step verification at the WORKER level would catch these BEFORE they hit loop-guard, saving budget and producing more actionable failure signals.

## Discipline (skill-level rule, not a mu-code primitive)

Add to `~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/SKILL.md` standing rules:

> **Per-step verification.** After any tool call whose success-state is observable, verify the result before consuming it downstream:
> - For `Edit`/`Write` returning an error: do NOT retry the same call. Read the error text; if it names a precondition (e.g. 'File has not been read yet'), satisfy it first.
> - For `Bash` returning non-zero exit AND empty output: investigate before retrying.
> - For tool results that decide control flow (e.g. `cargo test` output determining whether to close a bead): verify the success signal explicitly (exit code AND test-result line), don't just check that the command produced output.
> - For `Edit` operations that should land specific text: optionally Read the file post-Edit to confirm the change took.

This sits in tool-conventions.md as a calling-convention discipline, not in mu-code.

## Out of scope for this bead (filed separately if/when surfaces)

- A mu-code primitive that auto-runs verification after each tool call (a Tool-trait extension or wrapper). The skill-level discipline above is the immediate fix; a mu-code primitive is a longer-term substrate that would let any agent (not just /goal workers) get verification automatically.
- Verification of OUTPUT SCHEMA (LLM-generated structured output validation). Different beast from tool-result verification.

## Acceptance

- SKILL.md gains a 'Per-step verification' standing rule with the patterns above
- Next /goal experiment (could be mu-2w7 design exploration or mu-fb0 live-loop adoption) demonstrates the discipline — worker either avoids the pattern OR halts cleanly on verification failure
- task_log entry from the next experiment confirms whether the discipline reduced wasted-turn cost vs experiments 4-5 baseline

## References

- harness-engineering.ai compounding-failure math: 95%^20 = 36% end-to-end completion without verification; 96% WITH verification
- Vercel finding (same article): 'Removing 80% of tools available to the model got measurably better results' — supporting evidence that less context + tighter verification beats more options + silent retries
- Anthropic effective-harnesses article: 'self-verify all features. Only mark features as passing after careful testing'
- Our /goal experiments 4-5 (memory anchors via task_log queries on those dates) — empirical confirmation

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-14 after harness-audit pass through OpenAI / Anthropic / LangChain / harness-engineering.ai articles. Operator antagonistic check: this is the ONE pattern out of 7 candidate gaps where the argument is directly backed by our own experiment data — the others were articulate-but-not-actionable-yet.

title: TUI: arrow keys + Home/End/Backspace-word in prompt input mode
description:
## Why

In input mode (the prompt-typing affordance), only Backspace navigates — typed characters append, Backspace deletes the last char. There's no cursor movement: you can't arrow-left to fix a typo mid-prompt; the only path is to delete-back-to-the-typo. This is functional but feels broken because users have decades of muscle memory for readline-style editing.

Reported by user, 2026-05-11: "when trying to go back to change a typo, arrow keys aren't usable, so have to delete back to typo."

## What

Wire standard readline-ish keys into the input-mode handler in crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:

| Key | Action |
|---|---|
| Left / Right | Move cursor one char |
| Ctrl-Left / Ctrl-Right (or Alt-B / Alt-F) | Move cursor one word |
| Home / Ctrl-A | Move to start |
| End / Ctrl-E | Move to end |
| Ctrl-W | Delete previous word |
| Ctrl-U | Delete to start of line |
| Ctrl-K | Delete to end of line |
| Backspace | (already works) Delete previous char |
| Delete | Delete next char |

State change: replace `prompt_buffer: String` with a (String, cursor_position: usize) pair — or use a small struct. Insert / delete operations now respect the cursor position.

Rendering: show a cursor caret at the current position in the input field (ratatui's `Span::styled(" ", inverted)` for a block cursor, or use the terminal's native cursor positioning).

## Scope

NOT in scope here (separate bead): full hx integration via Ctrl-X Ctrl-E. That's its own bead (TUI: $EDITOR integration). This bead is the basic-readline-in-place fix.

## Acceptance

- Typing 'helo' then Left, Left, Left, 'l' yields 'hello'.
- Ctrl-A jumps to start of buffer; End jumps to end.
- Ctrl-W deletes the last whitespace-delimited word.
- Cursor visibly tracks position (block cursor, blinking or static).

## Estimate

~50-80 LOC in the input-mode handler + a small struct for cursor state, plus rendering tweaks. Well-trodden territory; `tui-input` and `tui-prompts` crates exist if 'wire it ourselves' feels redundant.

title: glob tool schema foot-gun — pattern alone doesn't glob; requires glob:true
description:
The `glob` tool's schema treats `{pattern: \"*\"}` differently from `{glob: true, pattern: \"*\"}`. Models call it with just `pattern` (the obvious shape) and get unexpected behavior; they retry with `glob: true` and it works. Wasted turn(s) every time.

## Symptom

Opus 4.7 session 2026-05-14, event log `03b75bc4a9c48f82`, first two tool calls:

```text
1. glob {\"kind\":\"any\", \"pattern\":\"*\"}                    ← malformed
2. glob {\"glob\":true, \"kind\":\"any\", \"pattern\":\"*\"}     ← retry, works
```

Operator initially read this as \"tried grep first then switched to glob\" — the failure was visible enough in the TUI to look like a different tool. The model self-corrected, but the foot-gun was a wasted call.

This is a recurring failure mode — any model trained on \"glob = match files\" will assume `pattern: \"*\"` globs. The current schema breaks that assumption.

## Why this is a schema bug, not a model bug

A tool whose schema accepts `{kind, pattern}` and silently treats those as something-other-than-glob when `glob:true` is missing is a tool whose schema is misaligned with its name. The fix is to either:

### A. Invert the default (recommended)

`glob:true` becomes the default. Add a `literal: bool` (default false) for the rare case where the operator wants literal-string matching. Models calling `glob { pattern: \"*\" }` get the obvious behavior.

### B. Make `glob` a required field

Tool schema rejects the call without `glob` set. Model gets a clear error, retries once. Better than silent misbehavior but adds a forced-retry to every glob call until the model learns the new shape.

### C. Rename the tool

If the current behavior with `glob:false` is the more common case, rename the tool to `find` or `match` and let glob be a separate tool. Highest churn; probably not worth it.

## Recommended fix: A

Glob tools should glob. The `literal` mode is the niche case.

## Scope

IN:
- Update `glob` tool's schema in `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/`: default `glob: true`; add `literal: bool` (default false).
- Update tool documentation in the schema description to reflect new defaults.
- Tests: existing tests that explicitly passed `glob: true` continue to work; new test confirms `pattern: \"*\"` alone now globs.

OUT:
- Schema migration for cached model contexts mentioning the old shape (those are session-local).
- Renaming to `find` (option C).

## Acceptance

- `glob { pattern: \"*\" }` globs by default.
- `glob { pattern: \"foo*\", literal: true }` does literal matching.
- Existing tests pass with updated shape.
- New regression test for the default behavior.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Symptom event log: `/home/tcovert/.local/share/mu/events/03b75bc4a9c48f82/session-1.jsonl`
- Tool location: `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/` (specific file TBD at impl time)

title: Subagent context handoff via shared read-only events + initial pointer-set
description:
Define how a parent session shares context with a spawned subagent without giving the subagent a mutable view of parent state.

## Mechanism

ChildSession { parent_session_id, parent_events_view: ReadOnlyEventLogHandle, initial_pointer_set: Vec<PointerToParentSpan>, own_event_log, own_retained_set (points into BOTH parent and own) }

Subagent receives at spawn: read-only access to parent event log; initial pointer-set; optional synthetic spans summarizing parent context. Writes only to its own log.

## Eviction semantics

Parent compaction drops a pointer to span S: parent's retained set no longer points at S; parent's event log STILL HAS S (append-only); if subagent retained its own pointer to S, the subagent's rope is unaffected. 'Eviction from parent = forgetting that pointer.' Parent compaction never strands subagent.

## Scope

In: ChildSession shape; spawn API; span ID namespace ((SessionId, EventId, SpanWithinEvent) triples); capability attenuation at spawn (subset of parent's allowed predicates); tests; doc.

Out: subagent dispatch UI; cross-subagent communication (mu-008/d22f391a — separate concern); multi-level subagent trees (start depth-1).

## Why

Today's options: copy parent context (expensive, stale) or give nothing (rebuild from brief — wasteful). Shared-read-only-events + initial-pointers: subagent has cheap parent access (read-only handle, not copy); independent retained set; provenance through both event logs.

## Acceptance

Child spawned with initial pointer-set has those pointers in retained rope; child's rendered AgentView includes parent-span content; parent compaction dropping span S does NOT affect child's retained pointer to S; child's event log distinct; doc covers shape + eviction + capability attenuation; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-5-subagent-context-handoff.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8, mu-nat
Memories: d22f391a, 5b69bcdf (cooperating-sessions mailbox — related, distinct)
Existing: event-sourced-context.md Session tree section

title: BUG: orphan mu serve / silent Popen hang under background-bash spawning
description:
Memory a1dd04fa has the full diagnostic pattern.

Symptom: Python orchestrator using MuClient hangs forever on fresh ask. No mu serve child appears in process tree; python sits in I (idle) syscall-state with ~0 CPU. No stderr. No exception.

Reproduces under Bash run_in_background=true especially with stale mu serve processes around. Foreground works.

Suspected mechanism: subprocess.Popen fork window stretching indefinitely under background-bash supervision. Possibly compounded by file-lock contention on ~/.config/mu/auth/openai-codex.json across daemons.

Workaround SHIPPED: mu_client.py defensive layer (jj qmlywmnm 186d5970, claude-personal scripts). read_timeout + on_progress + stderr drainer + orphan warning. Failures now surface as MuClientTimeout instead of silent stalls.

Real fix unclear without more diagnosis. Lower priority because workaround makes the failure visible rather than mysterious — but worth chasing when bandwidth allows.

title: mu-mcp: MCP (Model Context Protocol) plugin/tool-server support
description:
Filed 2026-05-13 after surveying Crush (Charmbracelet's coding agent), which lists MCP support as a first-class feature: 'Extensible: add capabilities via MCPs (http, stdio, and sse)'.

## What MCP is

Model Context Protocol (Anthropic-published spec). A wire protocol for agents to discover and call external tool servers. Three transport flavors: http (network), stdio (subprocess), sse (server-sent events). Decouples tool surface from agent binary.

## Why it'd matter for mu

Today mu's tool surface is built-in: bash, edit, grep, glob, read, write (in crates/mu-coding/src/tools/). Adding a new tool requires modifying the mu binary. MCP would let third-party tools register at session start (specified in config or session.delegate request).

## Relation to existing mu work

- **Capability-broker pattern** (memory 03b44633): MCP would be the WIRE surface; the capability broker is the SECURITY primitive. They compose — MCP tools get the same Capability-typed grants as built-in tools.
- **Skill activation**: mu's skill system already does dynamic capability registration via the rope (SkillActivation SpanKind, mu-ovl). MCP could be one shape of skill: 'this skill activates an MCP server and exposes its tools.'
- **AwsCapability** (mu-f5o): each MCP tool that touches AWS would need an attached AwsCapability for the broker to enforce. Same axis-based attenuation.

## Scope (when picked up)

IN (v1):
- MCP client implementation (probably stdio first — simplest transport, runs the tool server as a subprocess of mu serve)
- Tool registration during session setup
- Routing tool calls through the Tool trait surface mu already has
- Capability enforcement at the tool-dispatch layer (existing infrastructure)

OUT:
- HTTP/SSE transports (later, after stdio works)
- Built-in MCP server registry (operator manages config)
- Authentication / authorization beyond what Capability provides

## Trigger to actually pick this up

When a third-party tool (or a tool you want to write outside mu's tree) is needed. Today everything mu does is in-tree; until that pressure shows up, MCP is overkill.

## References

- MCP spec: https://modelcontextprotocol.io
- Crush's MCP integration: https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush (Go reference)
- Anthropic SDK MCP support: should already work in mu's anthropic provider — just need the client end to call/discover MCP tools
- Pattern shelf: mu-zh1 (Crush as the design reference)
- Security primitive: memory 03b44633 (capability_broker_pattern)
