title: BUG: cargo test --workspace fails intermittently — env-var mutation in tool tests races with parallel test execution
description:
## Symptom

`cargo test --workspace` fails non-deterministically; observed today (2026-05-11 ~19:00 local) with:

- tools::grep::tests::b4_count_mode
- tools::grep::tests::b6_case_insensitive

Same assertion: `assert!(!result.is_error)`. `cargo test -p mu-coding tools::grep` alone passes (11/11). Running b4 in isolation also passes. Task 19603420 reported all 278 workspace tests passing at 14:59 — the race window has just widened recently (more total tests = higher concurrency pressure).

## Root cause

`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/grep.rs::tests::rg_unavailable_returns_clean_error` (line 534) mutates the process-global `MU_RG_BINARY` env var:

```rust
std::env::set_var("MU_RG_BINARY", "/no/such/binary/rg");
// ... test runs ...
std::env::set_var("MU_RG_BINARY", original);  // or remove_var
```

Parallel `#[tokio::test]`s in the same test binary call `locate_rg()` which reads `MU_RG_BINARY`. Any test reading the var during the bogus-value window fails. b4 / b6 lose this race more often than b5; bash tests don't currently fail because their env keys (`MU_TEST_BASH_*`) don't interact with the rg path, but the same anti-pattern exists.

## Same anti-pattern elsewhere

`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/bash.rs` has two tests that mutate process env:

- tests::b5_strict_env_scrub (line 541): sets `MU_TEST_BASH_API_KEY`
- tests::b10_yolo_env_passes_through (line 598): sets `MU_TEST_BASH_YOLO_VAR`

These currently don't race against anything (no concurrent reader of those keys) but the pattern is fragile.

## Rust 1.74+ context

`std::env::set_var` is documented as not thread-safe and is `unsafe` from Rust 2024 edition onward. `cargo test` runs tests in threads by default. So any `set_var` in a test is a future-trap.

## Fix options

A. **Minimal (chosen for triage)**: `#[ignore]` the actual offender; document follow-up here. Loses one test path's coverage; that path ("rg not found") is structurally trivial. Done in commit landing alongside this bead.

B. **Durable**: refactor `locate_rg` so the rg binary path can be injected at `GrepTool` construction time. Tests pass a bogus path directly, no env mutation needed. Same shape applies to bash.rs (inject env scrub policy / passthrough policy as struct config rather than process env). Estimate: ~1 hour each.

C. **Standard fix**: add `serial_test` to workspace dev-deps; `#[serial]` the three offenders. Quick, adds one dep. Doesn't fix the root cause (env mutation in tests) — just serializes it.

## Suggested

Land A now (in this conversation's commit). Open follow-up beads referencing this one to do B for grep and B for bash separately.

title: mu-tui-bubbletea-reference: evaluate borrowing MVU / command / widget patterns from bubbletea-rs ecosystem
description:
Pattern-reference bead: evaluate whether mu's TUI could benefit from MVU / command / widget patterns from the bubbletea-rs Rust ecosystem. Not an adoption bead — the framework itself is pre-1.0 (v0.0.9 as of 2026-05-13) and mu's TUI is already substantial (~2800 LOC in main.rs alone, multiple open TUI beads).

## Reference

Repo: https://github.com/whit3rabbit/bubbletea-rs

Ecosystem:
- bubbletea-rs (v0.0.9) — core MVU framework, async runtime
- bubbletea-widgets (v0.1.12) — 13+ pre-built widgets (spinners, inputs, tables, progress, etc.)
- lipgloss-extras (v0.1.1) — styling DSL (colors, gradients, layouts)

Inspired by Charm's Bubble Tea (Go), which itself draws on Elm Architecture.

## What might be worth borrowing (NOT the framework wholesale)

1. **MVU pattern.** Pure-functional state transitions + commands that return future messages. If mu's TUI does not already follow MVU naturally, this is a cleaner refactor target than ad-hoc event loops.
2. **Command abstraction.** Async operations returning future messages instead of spawning tasks and threading channels. Could simplify how mu handles long-running async work in the TUI.
3. **Widget composition idioms.** How bubbletea-widgets composes widgets into a view tree could inform panel composition for mu-pex / mu-ib5 / mu-cha / mu-jb3.

## What argues against adoption

- bubbletea-rs is v0.0.9 — pre-stable, APIs explicitly stated to be "still evolving"
- mu's TUI is established and being actively developed (multiple open TUI beads)
- ratatui is the de facto Rust TUI; switching to a wrapper invites maintenance churn
- lipgloss-extras's styling is duplicative with ratatui's built-in Style/Modifier/Color

## Trigger to actually pick this up

When mu's TUI hits friction that MVU would clearly solve, or when starting a new substantial TUI surface where greenfield architecture choice is open. Until then: reference only.

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-13 after Thaddeus shared the link as 'don't know if it'd be useful to use some of the constructs.' Not urgent; placeholder so the reference doesn't get lost.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Additional TUI pattern reference (filed 2026-05-13 alongside bubbletea-rs): **oatmeal** — https://github.com/dustinblackman/oatmeal. Rust + ratatui terminal UI chat app for LLMs.

Specifically worth mining when working on:
- mu-pex / mu-jb3 / mu-ib5: panel/render TUI work — oatmeal's chat-bubble rendering is concrete reference for conversation display.
- Multi-backend provider abstraction: oatmeal's backend layer (ChatGPT / Ollama) is a smaller-scope analog of mu's provider abstraction.
- Slash command UX: oatmeal has slash commands; mu has Ctrl-X Ctrl-P (mu-62s); compare ergonomics.
- Neovim integration: oatmeal has a real Neovim plugin path. mu-tui currently has $EDITOR-handoff (Ctrl-X Ctrl-P) but no deeper editor integration. Could be a future bead direction.
- Themes: oatmeal supports themes. mu has color polish in mu-u8a (TUI color polish — moonfly/github_dark family).

This is reference material, not adoption material — like bubbletea-rs, oatmeal is its own application with its own architectural choices. Worth pattern-mining for specific surfaces, not wholesale framework borrowing.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Concrete widget candidate (filed 2026-05-13): **throbber-widgets-tui** — https://github.com/arkbig/throbber-widgets-tui. Direct fit for rendering mu-035's ProviderStatusSnapshot states as animated spinner+label. Filed as its own implementable bead (separate from this reference shelf) because it's a small concrete adopt-the-crate task, not pattern-mining. See mu-tui-throbber bead.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Three more ratatui showcase widgets surveyed 2026-05-13 (rapid-fire by operator browsing https://ratatui.rs/showcase/third-party-widgets/):

**1. tui-scrollview** — https://github.com/joshka/tui-widgets/tree/main/tui-scrollview
Simple scrollable views. Target beads where this directly fits:
- mu-ib5 (F9 mailbox view) — long mailbox lists almost certainly need scrolling
- Conversation history rendering generally (whatever mu-tui does today for long histories)

**2. tui-term** — https://github.com/a-kenji/tui-term
Embedded terminal emulator as a ratatui widget. Bigger architectural fit:
- Render mu's bash tool output as a LIVE terminal pane (vs. capturing stdout and rendering as text). Would let the user see commands execute in real-time, with proper terminal control sequence rendering (color, cursor, etc.).
- Could also replace the $EDITOR-handoff (Ctrl-X Ctrl-P, mu-62s) by embedding the editor in a pane instead of suspending the whole TUI.
- Bigger lift than a typical widget; worth its own design bead before commit.

**3. tui-tree-widget** — https://github.com/EdJoPaTo/tui-rs-tree-widget (or successor)
Tree view widget. **Direct fit for mu-cha (TUI: implement F2 session tree view)** — that bead is literally about rendering the session tree.

Pattern across all four widgets surfaced today (throbber + these three): ratatui's third-party widget ecosystem is rich enough that several open TUI beads have ready-made off-the-shelf primitives. Worth a sweep through the ratatui showcase before greenfield-building each bead.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Charmbracelet** (https://github.com/charmbracelet) — design-language reference (added 2026-05-13). All projects are Go; Rust ports exist but are third-party. Their projects ranked by mu-relevance:

- **Bubble Tea / Bubbles / Lip Gloss** (Go) — MVU framework / widgets / styling. Already covered: bubbletea-rs port noted earlier in this bead.
- **Glamour** (Go) — Markdown-in-terminal renderer. **Concrete fit for mu**: filed as separate bead (mu-tui-markdown) using termimad as the Rust analog.
- **VHS** (Go) — terminal recorder for making demo GIFs. Not on mu's critical path; relevant only if/when mu has a public README with demo content.
- **Mods** (Go) — AI on the command line. Adjacent to mu / claude-code / oatmeal but server-driven and Go-stack. Reference for product-shape ideas, not architecture.
- **Gum** (Go) — shell-friendly TUI commands. Not relevant to mu (mu is its own TUI, not a shell-script helper).

The brand pattern: Charmbracelet cultivates terminal aesthetic as a design language (consistent typography in banners, VHS-recorded GIFs, careful color palettes). Useful as design-philosophy reference even when not adopting specific libs.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Crush** (https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush) — most directly mu-shaped product Charmbracelet ships. Multi-model TUI coding agent (Go, built on their Bubble Tea / Lip Gloss / Glamour stack).

## Feature mapping to mu (current state + open beads)

| Crush | mu state |
|---|---|
| Multi-model (OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible + custom) | ✓ mu has anthropic / openai-codex / openrouter providers |
| Switch LLMs mid-session preserving context | mu-4ge (open, P3) — session.set_provider RPC. **Direct match.** |
| Session-based, multiple per project | ✓ mu has (mu-935 FileBackend, mu-cha session tree view) |
| LSP-enhanced context | mu-f6z **needs re-scoping** — crush uses LSP for *automatic context enrichment*, not just agent-queryable lookup. Bigger primitive than I originally captured. |
| MCP-extensible (http/stdio/sse) | **No mu equivalent.** Filed as new bead. |
| Cross-platform incl. BSDs | mu works on FreeBSD; BSD coverage otherwise untested |

## UX patterns worth studying (when looking at the demo)

- How mid-session model switch is surfaced in the UI (mu-4ge will need this decision)
- How LSP enhancement is exposed — silently inject context, or surface a 'context: file.rs:42 included' callout?
- How MCP tool registrations are listed / managed in-session
- Session-tree presentation (compare with mu-cha's planned F2 view)

## Worth noting

- Crush is Go; mu is Rust. Direct adoption isn't possible. Reference value is UX/architecture.
- They market 'Industrial Grade' / '25k+ applications' — claims to validate. Crush itself is newer.
- Their LSP-context-enrichment approach is the strongest take I've seen on 'agents should see semantic code'. Mu-f6z benefits from this framing.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Harness-audit catalog 2026-05-14** (field-awareness, NOT roadmap items):

Per OpenAI / Anthropic / LangChain / harness-engineering.ai articles, these are widely-cited harness primitives mu doesn't have. Filed here as awareness only — if symptoms surface later, the prior art is named:

- **Dynamic tool scoping per phase / skill** (Vercel: removing 80% of tools measurably improved performance). mu exposes all tools to every session; skills could gate. SkillActivation SpanKind exists; runtime loader doesn't.
- **Compaction runtime** for the rope. Compaction SpanKind landed in mu-ovl; auto-emission when context fills + demoting old spans isn't wired.
- **Tool output offloading**: head/tail kept in context, full output to filesystem. LangChain pattern. Useful for long-bash-output scenarios.

What we explicitly DIDN'T file as feature beads (after antagonistic-check pass):

- Checkpoint-resume with precondition validation — too abstract for now; events are the substrate; precondition revalidation is implementation-detail-ish
- Background gardening (recurring /goal cleanup runs) — useful but not yet needed at our scale
- AGENTS.md auto-injection — already covered in spirit by per-repo goal-protocol.md

The ONE thing we DID file as actionable: per-step verification discipline (P2) — see mu-(whatever the new ID is) — because the compounding-failure math (95%^20 = 36%) is concretely backed by our /goal experiment failures, not just article claims.
